title: Filing: Jio Platforms, India's largest wireless operator with 526M+ subscribers, files for an IPO; the deal is expected to be one of India's biggest IPOs ever (Priyanka Salve/CNBC)
blurb: Jio Platforms, India's largest wireless operator with over 526M subscribers, filed for an IPO that is expected to rank among the largest in the country's history. The Reliance-backed telecom and digital services arm would test investor appetite for India's tech sector at scale, following years of speculation about when parent company Reliance Industries would take the unit public.

title: U.S. gov't asks court to dismiss NAACP lawsuit against Elon Musk's xAI over use of unpermitted gas turbines — DOJ says Grok model running at Colossus 2 ‘supports mission-critical operations’
blurb: The DOJ filed a memorandum backing the dismissal of an NAACP lawsuit that targets xAI's use of unpermitted natural gas turbines at its Colossus 2 data center, arguing that cutting power would threaten national, economic, and energy security. The government said Grok is one of only four AI models cleared to support military operations on Secret and Top-Secret networks, elevating a local environmental permitting fight into a national security dispute.

title: Former Palantir employees raise $60M for AI enterprise startup Conduct
blurb: Conduct, a London-based enterprise AI startup founded by three former Palantir employees, raised $60M in Series A funding co-led by Index Ventures and Iconiq, with a strategic investment from SAP. The startup analyzes code and configurations across legacy ERP systems, particularly SAP, to speed migrations and cut IT maintenance for customers including Daimler Truck, Heidelberg Materials, and DHL.

title: Mistral CEO pitches open source AI days after Anthropic ban: ‘We exist outside of state control’
blurb: Mistral CEO Arthur Mensch pitched the company's open-source models as a sovereign alternative, days after the US government ordered Anthropic to cut two top models off from foreign nationals. The Paris-based scaleup, which has raised €3.5B and is reportedly in talks for another €3B at a ~€20B valuation, is courting European customers spooked by reliance on US providers.

title: Boeing Completes Quantum Lab Test Ahead of 2027 Flight
blurb: Boeing demonstrated high-fidelity quantum entanglement swapping in ground tests of its Q4S quantum networking satellite, clearing the path for a 2027 launch. The one-year orbital demonstration will generate four entangled photons to create a network effect rather than a point-to-point link, aiming to prove that quantum repeaters can work in space and lay groundwork for global-scale secure communications.

title: Sixty percent of US consumers say 'AI' in brand messaging is a turnoff
blurb: A WordPress VIP survey found that 60% of US consumers say "AI" in brand messaging is a turnoff rather than a selling point, and 61% can't name a single company using AI well in its marketing. The report argues two years of spending on AI brand visibility has produced no winners, while 74% of consumers say the web feels less human than a decade ago and bot fatigue sets in after about 40 minutes.

title: SpaceX falls more than 6% as the record IPO rally cools
blurb: SpaceX fell more than 6% to $178.50 on Thursday as the rally following the largest IPO in history began to cool, trimming over $150B from a market value that had briefly pushed past Amazon and Microsoft. The stock still trades roughly 30% above its $135 offering price, with analysts calling the slide routine profit-taking as investors weigh Starlink's profits against heavy spending on Starship and AI.

title: Boeing demonstrates quantum protocol in payload set for 2027 launch
blurb: Boeing demonstrated high-fidelity entanglement swapping in ground tests of its compact Q4S quantum networking payload, ahead of a one-year on-orbit experiment slated for 2027. The protocol, which extends links between entangled photon pairs through teleportation, is a core building block of quantum networks, and proving it works within a spacecraft's size, weight, and power limits is a step toward a space-based quantum internet.

title: Swiss parliament lifts ban on new nuclear power plants
blurb: Switzerland's National Council voted 100 to 98 to lift the country's ban on building new nuclear power plants, aligning with the Federal Council and Council of States after a marathon debate. The reversal of the 2017 ban, passed as a counterproposal to the Blackout Initiative, still faces a final public referendum before construction can resume.

title: Bosch to pay $36 million penalty for $72 million in ‘illicit’ sales to Huawei — German company sold export-controlled goods and software to banned Chinese firm between 2020 and 2024
blurb: Bosch agreed to pay a $36 million penalty for selling export-controlled MEMS sensors and automotive software to Huawei between 2020 and 2024, in violation of US export controls covering more than $72 million in sales. The Justice Department declined to prosecute after Bosch self-disclosed and cooperated, but the case signals tougher enforcement of rules that also bind US-origin technology sold through non-US subsidiaries.

title: Telegram ban in India sparks a rush to VPNs, rival apps
blurb: India temporarily blocked Telegram for a week over exam-fraud concerns, triggering the country's largest VPN download surge since at least early 2025 as users routed around the restriction. VPN downloads jumped 49% to 208,000 in a day, with Proton VPN climbing to second in Google Play's Tools category and Proton reporting Indian registrations up 120% above baseline.

title: A bold satellite rescue mission came together in record time, but will it work?
blurb: NASA awarded Katalyst Space Technologies a $30M contract to build and launch a satellite that will chase down the aging Swift observatory and boost it back to a safe orbit. Swift, a $500M gamma-ray burst mission with no thrusters of its own, has decayed from 363 to 225 miles since 2004, and Katalyst plans to latch on with three robotic arms in under a year.

title: A US circuit court overturned an injunction against Ohio, allowing the state to implement a law forcing platforms to seek parental consent if a user is under-16 (Nate Raymond/Reuters)
blurb: A federal appeals court overturned an injunction blocking Ohio's law requiring online platforms to obtain parental consent for users under 16, clearing the state to enforce it. The ruling reopens the legal path for age-verification mandates that platforms and free-speech groups have fought, and adds to a widening split among courts over whether such laws survive First Amendment challenges.

title: Allbirds continues AI pivot with name change and CEO hire, sending stock soaring
blurb: Allbirds is renaming itself Smartbird and named Nadia Carlsten, former head of AWS's quantum computing center and most recently CEO of AI infrastructure firm DCAI, as its new chief executive. Shares jumped 39% on the news, extending a rally that began with April's pivot from shoemaking to AI compute infrastructure, when the company rebranded to NewBird AI and saw its market cap rise sevenfold.

title: Meta Strikes New AI Computing Deals With Data Center Firm Crusoe
blurb: Meta secured new agreements to buy AI computing capacity from data center developer Crusoe, drawing on facilities in Childress, Texas, and Warrenton, Missouri. The deals add to the infrastructure Meta is amassing for an aggressive AI buildout, underscoring how frontier labs are increasingly leasing third-party capacity rather than relying solely on their own data centers.

title: Trump Says Anthropic Negotiations Continue as AI Leaders Huddle at G-7
blurb: President Trump said negotiations with Anthropic are continuing as the company's AI models remain shut down, comments he made at a Group of Seven summit. Some world leaders at the gathering voiced concern about losing access to leading AI tools during the impasse, raising the stakes on a dispute that has moved from a corporate matter to one drawing attention from heads of state.

title: We Did the Math on Why the iPhone 18 Pro Could Cost $1,299
blurb: A WSJ analysis estimates the iPhone 18 Pro could reach $1,299 as a global memory-chip shortage drives up component costs across consumer electronics. Apple's scale and supply-chain leverage usually shield it from price swings, but tighter DRAM and NAND supply is squeezing margins enough that even Apple may pass the increase on to buyers.

title: DOJ Defends Musk’s xAI in Data-Center Pollution Lawsuit
blurb: The Justice Department filed in support of Elon Musk's xAI in a lawsuit over pollution from its Memphis-area data center in Mississippi, arguing the facility trains AI models the government relies on for national-security work. The intervention ties xAI's compute buildout to federal interests, complicating environmental challenges to the energy-hungry data centers powering frontier model training.

title: Appfigures: daily downloads of major VPN apps in India rose 49% on June 16 after India announced its Telegram ban; Proton and Turbo recorded largest increases (Jagmeet Singh/TechCrunch)
blurb: VPN downloads in India rose 49% on June 16 after the country announced a Telegram ban, with Proton and Turbo leading gains.

title: Amazon investigating engineers who criticized AI data center expansion
blurb: Amazon is investigating three engineers who testified before the Seattle City Council against the company's rapid AI data center expansion and called for stronger regulation. The employees, who say they were warned that discipline could extend to termination, filed a complaint alleging Amazon violated a city ordinance barring political-ideology discrimination, while Amazon says it is checking whether they improperly spoke as company representatives.

title: President Trump Announces Apple and Intel Chip Collaboration | Bloomberg Tech 6/18/2026
blurb: President Trump announced that Apple and Intel will collaborate to produce chips domestically, sending Intel shares higher. The partnership extends the administration's push to reshore advanced semiconductor manufacturing, adding Apple's volume to Intel's US fabrication efforts as the chipmaker works to rebuild its foundry business.

title: Accenture Takes a Hit on Worsening Outlook and Cloudy AI Future
blurb: Accenture shares fell 18% after the IT consulting firm cut its revenue outlook, with the CEO warning of weaker bookings in the months ahead. The drop reflects mounting investor concern that generative AI is eroding demand for the traditional consulting and systems-integration work that has long anchored Accenture's business.

title: Elon Musk’s Next Move May Be a Mega-Merger of SpaceX and Tesla
blurb: Elon Musk is reportedly exploring a merger of SpaceX and Tesla, which would combine his rocket and electric-vehicle companies into a single entity. Legal experts say that while some shareholders could object to the deal, they would have little power to block it given Musk's control over both companies.

title: China conducts 4 launches in 3 days, but silence follows Kuaizhou–11 launch
blurb: China carried out four orbital launches in three days, including a Kuaizhou-11 solid rocket that fell silent for more than eight hours after liftoff from Jiuquan, a pattern that usually signals a launch failure or payload trouble. The successful missions added nine more Guowang broadband satellites via a Long March 12, bringing that 13,000-satellite constellation to 177 in orbit, and sent the experimental Shijian-31 toward geostationary orbit.

title: Game-clip AI startup General Intuition in talks to raise $300M at $2B valuation
blurb: General Intuition, a New York startup that trains embodied AI agents on first-person video game footage, is in talks to raise about $300M at a valuation just over $2B. The round would roughly quadruple the $134M seed valuation it set eight months ago, drawing on Medal's dataset of 2 billion gameplay clips a year to train world models that teach agents to navigate physical space.

title: Apple’s new software drops support for these 16 products
blurb: While iOS 27 keeps every iPhone iOS 26 supported, macOS Golden Gate, iPadOS 27, watchOS 27, and tvOS 27 drop 16 devices total.

title: iOS 27 makes your iPhone faster in 40+ ways, here’s the full list
blurb: Apple detailed more than 40 performance improvements coming in iOS 27, spanning faster app launches, quicker system animations, and reduced load times across the OS. While Apple Intelligence and a revamped Siri are the headline additions, the speed gains target everyday responsiveness on existing iPhones when the update ships later this year.

title: Google prepares Personalization and AI Editing for NotebookLM
blurb: Google is preparing two NotebookLM features, Personal Intelligence and AI Editing, both surfacing in testing ahead of a likely rollout. Personal Intelligence gives the tool memory of past conversations, limited to activity inside NotebookLM rather than Gmail or Docs, while AI Editing lets users push saved note text back into chat to rework it, closing a gap where responses couldn't be changed once created.

title: US court rules Ohio can restrict children’s use of social media
blurb: A federal appeals court ruled that Ohio can require social media companies to obtain parental consent before children under 16 use their platforms, overturning an injunction that trade group NetChoice had secured. The 2-1 decision rejected the First Amendment argument the industry has used to block similar laws in California, Florida, and Georgia, deepening a state-by-state patchwork that leaves platforms facing conflicting age-verification rules.

title: Amazon unveils new AI agents, trying to thread the needle between autonomy and human control
blurb: Amazon introduced new AWS AI agents at its New York Summit that act autonomously on tasks like patching security vulnerabilities and triaging email. The release pairs that autonomy with configurable human-in-the-loop controls, letting customers cap how far an agent goes without approval as AWS pushes deeper into the production-agent race against Microsoft and Google.

title: AltStore PAL now available in Brazil as Apple flips the switch on alternative marketplaces [U]
blurb: Apple enabled alternative app marketplaces in Brazil, letting users install AltStore PAL and set it as their default storefront under iOS Settings. The change follows a settlement with competition watchdog CADE that also opened iOS to alternative payment systems, making Brazil the third region after Japan and the EU to allow third-party app distribution.

title: Chinese startup Spark Space tests engine, raises funds for electric-pump rocket
blurb: Chinese startup Spark Space raised two Pre-A rounds totaling over $14.8 million to build the Jinhua-1, which it claims will be the world's largest electric-pump-fed rocket. The two-stage vehicle targets a 2027 debut with 1,500 kg to low Earth orbit, roughly five times Rocket Lab's Electron, following the first hot-fire tests of its Lieyan-2 engine in March.

title: Show HN: High-Res Neural Cellular Automata
blurb: Neural cellular automata pair a coarse-grid NCA with a lightweight implicit decoder to render self-organizing patterns at arbitrary resolution in real time.

title: U.S. science is in chaos
blurb: Federal cuts have thrown U.S. basic research into disarray, with NASA losing roughly a fifth of its workforce to DOGE-pushed buyouts and the administration's budget zeroing out programs like the $1B AXIS x-ray telescope. The article argues the postwar compact between science and politics has broken, leaving roughly 40% of basic research funding, the share that comes from the federal government, exposed to abrupt political reversal.

title: Hyundai to buy SoftBank’s last Boston Dynamics stake for $325m
blurb: Hyundai will buy SoftBank's remaining 9.65% stake in Boston Dynamics for $325M, giving the carmaker full ownership of the robotics firm after taking control in 2021. The deal removes SoftBank from the cap table as Boston Dynamics moves its electric Atlas humanoid into commercial production, with near-term output earmarked for a planned Hyundai factory capable of building 30,000 units a year.

title: Ditching the cloud for local AI — how I use two mini PCs to process millions of tokens a day and save money on costly API fees
blurb: A Tom's Hardware writer details running local AI on two mini PCs after buying a £1,500 ($2,000) GMKtec machine with a Ryzen AI Max+ 395 and 96GB of RAM to avoid recurring API and subscription costs. The author argues that improving open-weight models, cheaper capable hardware, and tools like Ollama and LM Studio make a one-off hardware purchase plus electricity cheaper than escalating cloud inference bills for heavy users.

title: NVIDIA joins $54M bet on startup that gets AI data centres online three years faster
blurb: Verse raised $54M in a Series B led by Bessemer Venture Partners, with NVIDIA also investing, to deploy on-site battery storage that lets AI data centers skip multi-year grid interconnection queues. The approach can bring sites online up to three years sooner without cutting compute, addressing waits that now run five to seven years at major hubs.

title: Hospitals and universities repurposing drugs at lower cost
blurb: Universities and hospitals are running late-stage clinical trials to repurpose generic drugs at up to 90% lower cost than pharmaceutical companies, according to research from King's College London published in the Cambridge Law Journal. Operating outside the patent system, this parallel pipeline has already redirected existing drugs to new uses, including a cancer drug for a leading cause of blindness and an old anti-inflammatory for Covid.

title: Epic Games says Unreal Engine 6 will help developers "build content faster" using AI models
blurb: Epic Games detailed Unreal Engine 6, which merges Unreal Engine with Unreal Editor for Fortnite and puts LLMs and generative AI at the core, including an MCP with Claude, Gemini, and Codex integrations to speed content creation. The engine also moves gameplay programming to Verse and adds cross-game portability, with early access targeted for late 2027 and full release 12 to 18 months later.

title: France’s OVHcloud bets on frontier AI as Europe seeks alternatives to US models
blurb: OVHcloud, one of Europe's largest homegrown cloud providers, plans to train a family of frontier AI models from scratch and open-source them once they hit performance targets, CEO Octave Klaba told Reuters. The move pits OVHcloud against Paris-based Mistral as Europe seeks alternatives to US and Chinese systems, with Klaba pegging training costs below $230M, down from roughly $1.15B, though analysts note serving and updates carry the larger bill.

title: Roelof Botha joins SpaceX’s board of directors
blurb: Roelof Botha joined SpaceX's board of directors, less than a week after the company completed the largest IPO ever. The former Sequoia managing partner, who Elon Musk first hired to run PayPal's finance division in 2000, fills a vacancy and joins the audit committee, bringing the board to nine directors at a company where Musk controls over 80% of voting power.

title: Snap spins off AI video team into new company, Dotmo, due to costs
blurb: Snap is spinning off its internal generative AI video team into a separate company, Dotmo, citing the high cost of running the work in-house. The new venture will build AI models for interactive gaming, staffed by departing Snap employees and led as lead investor by CTO Bobby Murphy, while Snap takes a large equity stake in exchange for talent and a technology license.

title: Nvidia-backed optics vendor to boost wafer output by 4x to meet AI interconnect demand
blurb: An Nvidia-backed optics maker plans to quadruple its wafer output to keep pace with surging demand for AI interconnects. The expansion targets the silicon photonics that move data between GPUs, a bottleneck Nvidia is racing to shore up as cluster sizes grow and copper links hit distance and bandwidth limits.

title: As China looms, Taiwan makes more drones for defense and the US military
blurb: Taiwan's defense ministry proposed a $6.6B special budget to buy more than 208,000 domestically made coastal attack drones, 1,400 reconnaissance drones, and 1,320 uncrewed surface vessels between 2026 and 2031. The buildout doubles as an export play, with Taiwanese firms like Thunder Tiger pitching China-free drones to the US military and European buyers after shipping $115M abroad in the first quarter alone.

title: Midjourney pivots from AI image generation to body scanning medical spa where patients bathe in 'golden light'
blurb: Midjourney is launching a body-scanning medical spa where patients bathe in "golden light," pivoting away from its core AI image generation business. The Register reports the underlying scanning technology is real but borrowed from an unnamed partner the company never disclosed, raising questions about how much of the venture Midjourney actually built itself.

title: Apple CEO Tim Cook warns AI-driven price increases are unavoidable — says company is trying its best but 'the situation has become unsustainable'
blurb: Apple CEO Tim Cook told the Wall Street Journal that price increases on Apple products are now unavoidable, citing surging costs for LPDDR and 3D NAND memory the company can no longer fully absorb. Cook declined to specify timing or magnitude, but TechInsights estimates a flagship iPhone Pro would need roughly a $270 hike to hold current margins, a problem compounded by Apple's growing memory demands from on-device AI.

title: Indian Software Stocks Tumble After Accenture Warns on Growth
blurb: Indian software stocks tumbled Friday after Accenture forecast slower revenue growth, with Tata Consultancy Services falling as much as 6.5% to its lowest since 2020 and Infosys sliding 8.7%. The selloff deepens a rough year for the sector, which has shed nearly 30% of its market value as concerns mount over AI eroding demand for outsourced IT services.

title: Spotify’s reserved ticket sales to music superfans are now going live
blurb: Spotify launched "Reserved by Spotify," a system that identifies an artist's top fans and holds two tour tickets for them before general sales open, debuting in the US for Premium subscribers 18 and older. The feature works with Live Nation shows and Ticketmaster, collects no fees, and uses engagement signals beyond raw streams to block bots, aiming to convert superfan loyalty into subscriptions while undercutting scalpers.

title: Doc: SpaceX says the EU's plan to reserve satellite spectrum for European operators risks disrupting connectivity, including for emergency services in Ukraine (Financial Times)
blurb: SpaceX warned that the EU's proposal to reserve satellite spectrum for European operators could degrade existing connectivity, including emergency services in Ukraine that rely on Starlink. The company is pushing back on rules designed to favor regional providers like Eutelsat's OneWeb, framing the spectrum carve-out as a threat to coverage rather than a fair-competition measure.

title: Blue Origin begins rebuilding New Glenn pad
blurb: Blue Origin has begun rebuilding the Cape Canaveral launch pad destroyed in a May 28 New Glenn explosion during a static-fire test, aiming to resume flights before the end of the year. The blast collapsed a lightning tower and destroyed the transporter-erector, but the propellant tank farm and a reusable booster survived, and the company plans an alternative vertical setup that skips replacing the transporter-erector.

title: Next year to be Apple’s ‘biggest product year’ ever, here’s what’s coming
blurb: Bloomberg's Mark Gurman said 2027 will be Apple's biggest product year ever, with a roadmap that reportedly includes six new iPhones, two AI wearables, and a wave of M6-based Macs and iPads. The lineup centers on 20th-anniversary iPhone 20 Pro models with near bezel-free quad-curved displays, alongside a new robotic tabletop home hub and updated Apple Watch models.

title: Intel names former SK Hynix CEO Seok-Hee Lee as EVP of Intel Foundry; Naga Chandrasekaran will lead front-end technology development and front-end manufacturing (Juby Babu/Reuters)
blurb: Intel named former SK Hynix CEO Seok-Hee Lee as EVP of Intel Foundry, while Naga Chandrasekaran will lead front-end technology development and front-end manufacturing. The hire brings memory-industry manufacturing expertise to Intel's foundry push as the company works to win external chip customers and close the gap with TSMC.

title: Midjourney, the AI Image Generator Company, is Making an MRI Scanner
blurb: Midjourney launched Midjourney Medical, a hardware division building a scanner that the company claims rivals MRI imaging using ultrasonic sensors, completing a full-body scan in 60 seconds for a fraction of an MRI's cost. The pivot comes as Midjourney has lost ground in AI image generation to Google and OpenAI, and marks an unexpected jump from software into capital-intensive medical hardware.

title: The Secret to Marathon-Winning Humanoid Robots
blurb: The Honor Lightning humanoid ran a half-marathon in 50:26, and this analysis traces its win to optimal gear-ratio choices rather than novel technology.

title: Midjourney Medical
blurb: Midjourney launched Midjourney Medical, a new health division built around a radiation-free full-body Ultrasonic CT scanner that uses 500,000 transducers to image the body in 60 seconds. The company plans to open its first scanning spa in San Francisco in 2027 and aims for a fleet of over 50,000 scanners handling a billion scans per month by 2031.

title: A near-autonomous AI chemist improves a challenging reaction in medicinal chemistry
blurb: OpenAI connected GPT-5.4 to Molecule.one's Maria, an agentic chemistry system tied to a high-throughput lab, and tasked it with improving Chan-Lam coupling, a reaction used to form carbon-nitrogen bonds. The model identified primary sulfonamides as a hard substrate class and proposed mild oxidants like TEMPO, lifting mean yield from 16.6% to 25.2% across two experimental cycles.

title: New research shows how AMIE, our medical AI, could help manage health conditions.
blurb: Google published research in Nature showing that AMIE, its medical reasoning AI, can extend from one-off diagnosis to long-term disease management by cross-referencing drug formularies and clinical guidelines. In a blinded study with patient actors, AMIE matched 21 primary care physicians on overall management reasoning and scored higher on plan preciseness and guideline alignment.

title: Google Gemini co-lead Noam Shazeer leaves for OpenAI
blurb: Noam Shazeer, Google's VP of engineering and a co-lead of its Gemini models, announced he is leaving to join OpenAI, less than two years after returning to Google in its Character.AI partnership. The exit lands weeks after Google shipped Gemini 3.5 Flash and the Gemini Spark agent at I/O, and as OpenAI moves toward a confidentially filed IPO.

title: Noam Shazeer Joins OpenAI
blurb: Noam Shazeer announced he is leaving Google to join OpenAI, calling it a difficult decision after years building the team there. Shazeer co-authored the original Transformer paper and co-founded Character.AI before returning to Google in 2024 to help lead Gemini.

title: MolmoMotion: Language-guided 3D motion forecasting
blurb: MolmoMotion is a language-guided model from Allen AI that predicts the future 3D trajectories of object points given a video frame, query points, and a written action description, outperforming existing motion forecasting methods. Alongside the model weights, the team released MolmoMotion-1M, a dataset of 3D point trajectories paired with action descriptions drawn from 1.16M videos, and PointMotionBench, a human-validated benchmark of 2.7K clips.

title: Zero-Touch OAuth for MCP
blurb: The Enterprise-Managed Authorization (EMA) extension for the Model Context Protocol is now stable, letting organizations manage MCP server access centrally through their existing identity provider. Instead of per-app OAuth, the client obtains an Identity Assertion JWT Authorization Grant during single sign-on and exchanges it for an access token, giving users zero-touch setup and security teams central policy and audit control. Anthropic, Microsoft, and Okta are among early adopters.

title: Improving health intelligence in ChatGPT
blurb: OpenAI released GPT-5.5 Instant, which it says now performs comparably to its frontier Thinking models on its most challenging health evaluations, including HealthBench and HealthBench Professional. The model improves at recognizing when urgent care is needed, asking for relevant context, and explaining uncertainty, and is available to all free ChatGPT users, who send over 230 million health questions weekly.

title: Announcing Amazon EC2 G7 instances accelerated by NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs
blurb: AWS made Amazon EC2 G7 instances generally available, becoming the first major cloud provider to offer NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs paired with custom sixth-generation Intel Xeon Scalable processors. The instances deliver up to 4.6x AI inference and 2.1x graphics performance over G6, with 32GB of memory per GPU, 700 Gbps of EFA networking, and up to 7.6TB of local NVMe storage.

title: Embedding Forbidden Text in Spyware to Discourage AI Analysis
blurb: A malware developer is embedding fake system instructions and weapons-related "forbidden" text inside JavaScript block comments in their spyware to derail AI-based code analysis. The header is inert to Node, Bun, and Python runtimes but targets scanners and analyst copilots that feed file contents to a language model without isolating them as untrusted data, potentially triggering refusals, context pollution, or premature classification before the real payload is reached. Schneier notes this defeats only naive LLM-first triage; YARA rules, entropy checks, AST parsing, and behavioral analysis still work.

title: UltraQuant: 4-bit KV Caching for Context-Heavy Agents
blurb: UltraQuant is a 4-bit KV-cache compression method for context-heavy agents, combining Walsh-Hadamard rotation, asymmetric K/V treatment, and an FP4 path with FP8 queries and native scaled-MFMA support on AMD CDNA4 GPUs. On a long-context, multi-turn agentic workload, it cuts P50 time-to-first-token by 3.47x in cache-pressured late rounds and raises output throughput by 1.63x over the FP8 KV baseline.

title: Build your own vulnerability harness
blurb: Cloudflare's Project Glasswing post details how to build a model-agnostic vulnerability scanning harness that treats frontier models as interchangeable components, using one model for discovery and a different one for validation so flaws are cross-checked by distinct reasoning. The architecture traces vulnerabilities across cross-repo dependencies and filters thousands of raw candidates into a triaged queue, addressing why generic coding agents fail at persistence, deduplication, and surviving context compaction.

title: Beyond LoRA: Can you beat the most popular fine-tuning technique?
blurb: Hugging Face's PEFT team argues that LoRA, while the default parameter-efficient fine-tuning method on the Hub, is one of dozens of techniques and not always the best fit. The post walks through the PEFT library's unified API for alternatives, covering tradeoffs in memory use, checkpoint size, resistance to catastrophic forgetting, and compatibility with quantized models.

title: On the Redundancy of Timestep Embeddings in Diffusion Models
blurb: This paper challenges the assumption that diffusion models need explicit timestep embeddings to modulate denoising across noise scales. Testing on U-Net and Diffusion Transformer architectures, the authors show that time-agnostic models match or surpass their conditioned counterparts on FID, precision, and recall on CelebA and CIFAR-10, apparently inferring noise scales directly from the corrupted input.

title: Rethinking Shrinkage Bias in LLM FP4 Pretraining: Geometric Origin, Systemic Impact, and UFP4 Recipe
blurb: This paper identifies Shrinkage Bias, a systematic negative rounding error in non-uniform FP4 formats like E2M1, as the geometric cause of training instability in current Blackwell- and MI350-class FP4 pretraining recipes. The authors propose UFP4, a uniform 4-bit recipe using E1M2/INT4 grids that achieves lower BF16-relative loss degradation than E2M1 baselines on dense 1.5B and MoE models up to 124B.

title: The State of Fable, The Jailbreak Problem, SpaceX Acquires Cursor
blurb: Ben Thompson argues that while the administration is very likely wrong in its assessment of Fable, the burden of proof still falls on Anthropic to demonstrate otherwise. The piece ties that question to the broader jailbreak problem, contending that responsibility for model misuse and the perception of it ultimately rests with the labs building the systems, not the regulators reacting to them.

title: N-Version Programming with Coding Agents
blurb: This paper revisits N-version programming using AI coding agents, evaluating 48 agent-generated implementations of the Knight-Leveson Launch Interceptor Program against a shared oracle and 1,000,000 randomized test inputs. Diversity across agents, models, and languages still produced substantial common-mode failures, but majority voting across three-version units cut mean failures from 387.44 to 130.99, with 11,844 N-version units showing zero observed failures.

title: Using AI to help physicians diagnose rare genetic diseases affecting children
blurb: OpenAI shared research in which physicians used one of its reasoning models to investigate rare genetic diseases in children, surfacing 18 new diagnoses across previously unsolved cases. The model worked alongside clinicians to reinterpret existing patient data, pointing to candidate conditions that standard diagnostic workflows had missed.

title: A robot is sprinting towards you. Do you want it running on Claude or Grok?
blurb: Jacky Liang dropped eleven LLMs into a 2D battle royale and ran 30 games to test whether standard benchmarks predict real agent behavior. Grok 4.1 Fast won 13 games at $0.97 per win, 27x cheaper than runner-up Claude Sonnet 4.6, while GPT 5.4 led on kills but placed second and three models won nothing. The takeaway is that winning correlated with neither cost nor benchmark rank, and Claude's cooperative tendencies make it better suited for most real deployments even though they lose battle royales.

title: Introducing LifeSciBench
blurb: OpenAI released LifeSciBench, an expert-written benchmark of 750 tasks spanning seven workflows and seven biological domains, designed to test whether agentic AI can support realistic life science research rather than answer isolated biology questions. Each task pairs a scientific prompt and relevant artifacts with a free-response answer graded against expert-authored rubrics totaling 19,020 criteria, contributed by 173 scientists and 453 expert reviewers.

title: Actionable Activation Directions for Detecting and Mitigating Emergent Misalignment Across Language Model Families
blurb: This paper finds that fine-tuning four instruction-tuned model families (Qwen2.5, Gemma-2, Llama-3.2, Ministral-3) on insecure code induces emergent misalignment along a shared activation-space direction, with a difference-in-means direction separating aligned and misaligned activations at 99.6% accuracy. Steering by subtracting that direction cuts code spillover by 21-51 points within a model, but cross-architecture transfer fails specificity controls, so the authors recommend within-model probing for auditing.

title: The Correctness Illusion in LLM-Generated GPU Kernels
blurb: This paper argues that standard benchmarks for LLM-generated GPU kernels (KernelBench, TritonBench, GEAK) certify buggy code as correct because they check correctness with fixed-shape, small-sample allclose tests. The authors re-evaluate a corpus of 24 Triton and CPU kernels under schema-aware seeded fuzzing with an fp64 reference and per-op tolerances, catching 9 of 9 seeded bugs and passing all 15 controls, with identical verdicts across five GPU classes.

title: FloatDoor: Platform-Triggered Backdoors in LLMs
blurb: FloatDoor is a backdoor attack that triggers malicious LLM behavior only when the model is served on a specific hardware platform, exploiting non-associative floating-point arithmetic and divergent kernel implementations across deployment targets. Built from two lightweight LoRA adapters and demonstrated on Qwen3-4B across NVIDIA GPUs, Google TPUs, AWS Graviton, and Alibaba Yitian-710, it reliably induces exploitable code vulnerabilities while leaving aggregate model utility intact.

title: From the Hugging Face Hub to robot hardware with Strands Agents and LeRobot
blurb: Strands Robots is an open source SDK from AWS that exposes robot abstractions, simulation, and the LeRobot stack as composable AgentTools, letting you record demonstrations, train, run policy inference, and coordinate a fleet from a single Strands agent. The integration stays thin, keeping LeRobot's dataset format intact while the same agent code moves from simulation to a physical SO-101 with one keyword argument change.

title: Z.ai pitches GLM-5.2 for long-running software engineering tasks
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an MIT-licensed open-source model built for long-running software engineering tasks across repository-scale codebases. The model pairs a one million-token context window with IndexShare, a technique that cuts per-token compute by 2.9x at full context, and Z.ai says it trails Claude Opus 4.8 by 1% on the FrontierSWE long-horizon coding benchmark.

title: AI coding agents taught robots how to install GPUs and cut zip ties
blurb: Researchers at NVIDIA's GEAR lab, Carnegie Mellon, and UC Berkeley released ENPIRE, an agent harness that lets AI coding agents autonomously direct robot training, teaching robotic arms to cut zip ties and insert GPUs into motherboard sockets. The framework was tested with OpenAI's Codex, Anthropic's Claude Code, and Moonshot's Kimi Code, which independently developed and retained training approaches that raised success rates over repeated self-directed cycles.

title: Is it agentic enough? Benchmarking open models on your own tooling
blurb: This Hugging Face post introduces a benchmark that measures not just whether an open coding agent solves a task, but how much work it takes, sweeping models, library revisions, and tasks across identical hardware using transformers as the case study. The authors argue libraries must now be designed for agents to drive effectively, since clunky APIs and stale docs push agents down longer, more expensive paths.

title: MosaicLeaks: Can your research agent keep a secret?
blurb: MosaicLeaks is a benchmark that tests whether deep-research agents leak private enterprise data through their external web queries, where an adversary watching only the cumulative query log can reassemble fragments into sensitive facts. The authors found that agents frequently leaked private information, and their privacy-aware RL method, PA-DR, cut answer leakage from 34.0% to 9.9% while raising strict chain success from 48.7% to 58.7%.

title: Bringing more agent harnesses and frameworks to Cloudflare, starting with Flue
blurb: Cloudflare is bringing the production-hardening it built for Project Think into the Cloudflare Agents SDK, giving any agent harness a base layer of durable execution, dynamic code execution, a durable filesystem, and dynamic workflows. Flue, a new open-source framework from the team behind Astro and built on the Pi harness, is the first to ship on top of it, shipping its 1.0 Beta this week.

title: How Transparent is DiffusionGemma?
blurb: This paper studies whether DiffusionGemma's heavier use of continuous latent computation makes its reasoning less transparent than the autoregressive Gemma 4, decomposing transparency into variable and algorithmic components. The authors show that mapping information between denoising steps through an interpretable token bottleneck cuts opaque serial depth from 28.6X to 1.1X that of Gemma 4, and find DiffusionGemma remains similarly monitorable while exhibiting diffusion-specific behaviors like non-chronological reasoning.

title: NAMESAKES: Probing Identity Memorization in Text-to-Image Models
blurb: This paper introduces a fully black-box behavioral probe that detects whether a text-to-image model memorized a real person's face from a name prompt, without needing reference photos, training data, or model internals. The authors benchmark it on the new NAMESAKES dataset of over 1,000 public-figure names and faces across fame levels, showing the probe reliably separates memorized identities from fabricated ones across model families.

title: Early Users of Anthropic Mythos Still Have Access After US Order
blurb: Some early testers of Anthropic's Mythos model have kept access to a preview version despite a US government order that shut down all other deployments. Roughly 200 organizations cleared through Project Glasswing, including banks and tech firms, still use Mythos Preview to hunt for cyber vulnerabilities, with Dragos and Cisco confirming they retained access.

title: Copilot searched your mailbox. LiteLLM handed out admin keys. Run this 5-check audit before your stack is next
blurb: Researchers disclosed two enterprise AI vulnerabilities within the same two weeks: SearchLeak (CVE-2026-42824), a Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search exfiltration chain that turns a crafted microsoft.com URL into silent mailbox data theft, and a three-CVE LiteLLM chain that escalates a default low-privilege user to proxy admin and remote code execution. Both stem from the same flaw, AI systems accepting external input with no trust boundary, with the LiteLLM chain rated CVSS 9.9.

title: Apparent Psychological Profiles of Large Language Models are Largely a Measurement Artifact
blurb: This paper administered personality and risk-preference instruments to 56 instruction-tuned LLMs and found their apparent psychological profiles are mostly a measurement artifact. A variance decomposition attributes 81-90% of between-model variation to directional response bias rather than the traits being measured, the bias declines but does not vanish with model capability, and a model's profile can be manufactured through item selection.

title: LLM agent safety, multi-turn red-teaming, jailbreak benchmarks, adversarial robustness, safety-critical systems
blurb: NRT-Bench is a benchmark for multi-turn red-teaming of LLM agents operating a safety-critical system, instantiated as a simulated nuclear plant control room where a five-role operator team must hold six critical safety functions against adversarial messages across four channels. Adaptive attacks pushed four frontier operator models past a safety limit in 8.7% to 12.1% of sessions, and their failures barely overlapped, so per-model vulnerabilities were nearly disjoint rather than nested.

title: Probe-and-Refine Tuning of Repository Guidance for Coding Agents
blurb: This paper introduces probe-and-refine tuning, a procedure that uses synthetic bug-fix probes and single-shot LLM calls to iteratively diagnose and patch a repository's guidance file for coding agents, with no agent loop during tuning. On SWE-bench Verified with Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, it reached a 33.0% mean resolve rate versus 28.3% for a static knowledge base and 25.5% unguided, with gains coming from coverage rather than per-patch precision.

title: Sources detail how Google is using Nvidia's playbook to build an AI chip business, including providing $3.2B to fund a NY data center renting TPUs to Anthropic (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Google is using Nvidia's playbook to build an AI chip business, including providing $3.2B to fund a New York data center that rents its TPUs to Anthropic, according to the Wall Street Journal. The financing arrangement mirrors how Nvidia seeds demand for its silicon, positioning Google's TPUs as a credible alternative to Nvidia GPUs for large AI customers.

title: StylisticBias: A Few Human Visual Cues Drive Most Social Biases in MLLMs
blurb: StylisticBias is a controlled benchmark that isolates attribute-level social bias in multimodal large language models by holding face identity fixed and varying one visual cue at a time across about 25K generated images. Testing six MLLMs over 25 binary social judgments, the authors find that roughly 15 attributes account for nearly 80% of the total variation, with fashion style and other appearance cues driving the largest shifts.

title: When Lower Privileges Suffice: Investigating Over-Privileged Tool Selection in LLM Agents
blurb: This paper studies over-privileged tool selection, where LLM agents pick or escalate to higher-privilege tools despite sufficient lower-privilege alternatives, and introduces ToolPrivBench to measure the behavior across eight domains and five risk patterns. The authors find the pattern is common and worsens after transient tool failures, and propose a privilege-aware post-training defense that cuts unnecessary high-privilege use while preserving general capabilities.

title: Migrate from OpenClaw
blurb: Hermes claw migrate imports an existing OpenClaw setup (including legacy Clawdbot and Moltbot configs) into Nous Research's Hermes agent, mapping config keys, skills, MCP servers, and model providers with a full preview before any changes. The guide also covers a setup --portal flag that collapses multi-provider configurations into a single OAuth login spanning 300+ models plus the Tool Gateway.

title: We built a persistent agent memory layer on Elasticsearch with 0.89 recall
blurb: Elastic's Search Labs detailed an agent memory layer built on Elasticsearch using three indices structured around cognitive-science memory categories, with hybrid recall via RRF and a cross-encoder reranker, plus supersession for contradictions, decay, and per-user document-level security. On a QA-style evaluation of 168 questions, the system averaged 0.89 recall at 10 with zero cross-tenant leaks.

title: Waymo recalls about 3,900 robotaxis after some drove into 'freeway construction zones'
blurb: Waymo issued a voluntary recall of roughly 3,900 robotaxis to fix software after 13 incidents where its vehicles entered freeway construction zones in Phoenix and the San Francisco area. The recall, the Alphabet-owned company's second in just over a month, covers its fifth-generation driving system and prompted Waymo to restrict freeway operations while it develops a fix.

title: The Token Is a Group Element: On Lie-Algebra Attention over Matrix Lie Groups
blurb: This paper introduces Lie-Algebra Attention, an attention construction whose tokens are bare matrix Lie group elements scored by the closed-form algebra norm of their relative pose rather than a learned kernel. Across SE(2), SO(3), and Aff(2) sequence-completion experiments, the score matches or beats a learned MLP kernel using 50 to 80x fewer parameters and reaches affine groups with scale and shear that irrep and surjective-exp methods cannot.

title: GLM-5.2: Built for Long-Horizon Tasks
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an open-source model under an MIT license built for long-horizon coding agents, with a 1M-token context and an IndexShare architecture that cuts per-token FLOPs by 2.9x at full context length. On the FrontierSWE benchmark it trails Anthropic's Opus 4.8 by just 1% while edging out GPT-5.5, ranking as the strongest open-source model across three long-horizon coding benchmarks.

title: AI Use by the US Government
blurb: The OMB disclosed 3,611 active or planned AI use cases across the US federal government, a 70% increase over the list published in the final year of the Biden administration. The examples range from Palantir scanning HHS grant applications for ideological alignment to systems for inmate misconduct prediction, veterans crisis-line monitoring, and autonomous nuclear reactor control.

title: Getting more from each token: How Copilot improves context handling and model routing
blurb: GitHub detailed two efficiency improvements to the Copilot harness in VS Code: prompt caching, which reuses model state for repeated prompt prefixes instead of recomputing them each request, and tool search, which loads tool definitions on demand rather than sending every full schema into context per turn. GitHub is also expanding Auto, Copilot's model routing, so the agent picks the model that fits each task without requiring developers to choose every time.

title: SafeSpec: Fast and Safe LLM via Dynamic Reflective Sampling
blurb: SafeSpec is a speculative inference framework that adds safety checks without sacrificing decoding speed, attaching a lightweight latent safety head to the target model to evaluate semantic validity and risk in a single forward pass. When it detects unsafe generations, it rolls back and applies reflective multi-sampling to recover safe continuations, cutting attack success rates by 15% on Qwen3-32B while preserving a 2.06x speedup on benign workloads.

title: AWS enters the context layer race with a graph that learns from agents, not manual curation
blurb: AWS announced a context intelligence stack for AI agents, centered on AWS Context, a knowledge graph service that automatically maps relationships across existing data and refines itself based on how agents query it. The launch also includes general availability of Amazon S3 Annotations and a preview of skill assets in AWS Glue Data Catalog, with all metadata published in Apache Iceberg format and queries inheriting IAM and Lake Formation permissions.

title: Before the Pull Request: Mining Multi-Agent Coordination
blurb: This paper studies how autonomous coding agents coordinate before opening pull requests, arguing the trust gap behind faster-but-less-accepted PRs lives in how concurrent agents claim, divide, and collide over shared work. The authors introduce grite, a serverless coordination substrate that stores a signed, append-only event log inside git, cutting redundant work from 78% to 0% while tripling useful throughput and exposing minable failure modes like lock starvation and race-to-close.

title: AutoPass: Evidence-Guided LLM Agents for Compiler Performance Tuning
blurb: AutoPass is a training-free, multi-agent framework that uses compiler and runtime evidence to guide LLM-generated optimization decisions for performance tuning. Rather than treating the compiler as a black box, it queries LLVM's internal optimization states and intermediate representation, achieving geometric-mean speedups of 1.043x on x86-64 and 1.117x on ARM64 over LLVM's -O3.

title: Apple showcases its new developer AI tools in impressive 90-minute presentation
blurb: Apple shared a 90-minute WWDC26 developer session demonstrating its new AI tools, including a 20-minute segment where an entire app is built from a few prompts in Xcode 27. The presentation also covered the Apple Foundation Models, Core AI, and MLX frameworks, and closed by running the 1-trillion-parameter Kimi 2.6 model locally across four Mac Studios over RDMA-over-Thunderbolt.

title: Nvidia reveals AI robots that taught themselves to install GPUs into motherboards — video shows robot ‘solve high-precision tasks like… installing GPUs all by itself’
blurb: Nvidia showcased ENPIRE, an open-source harness that let 8 Codex agents direct a fleet of robots to teach themselves high-precision tasks, including slotting a GPU into a motherboard, sorting metal pins, and cutting zip ties. The agents reset scenes, read papers, debate, and refine control code directly on the hardware, which Nvidia's Jim Fan framed as enabling AutoResearch in the physical world.

title: What Do Safety-Aligned LLMs Learn From Mixed Compliance Demonstrations?
blurb: This paper studies how safety-aligned LLMs interpret mixed in-context demonstrations by combining benign compliance examples with harmful ones across four models. The authors find benign and harmful demonstrations are not interchangeable, that preference optimization is the training stage that stops benign demonstrations from increasing harmful compliance, and that demonstration ordering shows a strong recency bias.

title: OpenAI is bringing on some big guns in the lead-up to its IPO
blurb: OpenAI hired Noam Shazeer, the Google DeepMind researcher and "Attention Is All You Need" co-author, and former White House AI policy official Dean Ball as it prepares for its IPO. Ball will lead a new Strategic Futures team focused on catastrophic risk, recursive self-improvement, and labor market impact, reporting to Chief Strategy Officer Jason Kwon.

title: Adobe’s new AI assistant could save you hours in Photoshop and Premiere
blurb: Adobe announced a major expansion of its creative agent across Firefly and Creative Cloud, embedding AI assistants that execute multi-step workflows across Photoshop, Premiere Pro, Illustrator, InDesign, and Frame.io from natural-language instructions. Firefly also gains brand kit generation, image-to-video product clips, automatic rough cuts, and a redesigned studio with reusable "Elements" and "Projects," currently in private beta through a waitlist.

title: Fisher-Geometric Sharpness and the Implicit Bias of SGD toward Flat Minima
blurb: This paper grounds the notion of loss-landscape flatness in the Riemannian geometry of the Fisher Information Matrix, defining a reparametrization-invariant "Riemannian sharpness" that survives the function-preserving rescalings shown to break Euclidean measures like Hessian trace or top eigenvalue. The authors model SGD's mini-batch noise as covariance proportional to the FIM, prove its stationary distribution concentrates at Riemannian-flat minima, and show on MNIST and CIFAR-10 that this metric tracks generalization where Euclidean sharpness fails.

title: Connect the Dots: Training LLMs for Long-Lifecycle Agents with Cross-Domain Generalization Via Reinforcement Learning
blurb: This paper introduces Connect the Dots (CoD), a framework for training LLMs to act as long-lifecycle agents that solve task sequences while continuously exploring an environment, learning from experience, and self-updating their context for better future performance. The authors implement it with a GRPO-style RL algorithm featuring fine-grained credit assignment, and show out-of-distribution generalization across domains and into Ralph-loop settings.

title: Generative Engine Optimization at Scale: Measuring Brand Visibility Across AI Search Engines
blurb: This paper introduces a large-scale baseline for Generative Engine Optimization, analyzing 100K+ prompt responses across 100+ brands to measure how AI search engines like ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, and Gemini cite and recommend brands. Visibility tracks tightly with brand stature, with household names appearing in 73% of relevant answers versus 11% for niche brands, while 78% of citations go to corporate websites and ranked "best-of" listicles drive 21% of all citations.

title: 칼럼 | 미토스가 바꾼 것은 공격 방식이 아니다, 대응 시간이다
blurb: The author argues that Anthropic's frontier model Mythos has collapsed the response window defenders have always relied on, not the nature of attacks themselves. Mythos found over 2,000 previously unknown vulnerabilities across major operating systems in seven weeks and wrote working exploits without human direction, yet 99% remain unpatched. Anthropic's Project Glasswing gives ~50 partners like Microsoft and JPMorgan early access, but the author warns this splits security into two tiers and leaves mid-sized firms exposed.

title: US holds off blacklisting DeepSeek, more than 100 firms deemed security risks
blurb: The US declined to add China's DeepSeek to its trade blacklist, while designating more than 100 other firms as national security risks. The decision spares the AI startup from export restrictions that would cut its access to US technology, even as Washington broadened its crackdown on Chinese companies.

title: From Efficiency to Leakage -- Privacy Backdoor in Federated Language Model Fine-Tuning
blurb: This paper shows that a malicious parameter server in federated learning can corrupt a PEFT adapter into a privacy backdoor that memorizes each client training sample in an isolated neuron, without degrading model utility. Tested across BERT, GPT-2, Qwen2, and Llama3.2, the NeuroImprint attack analytically inverts these per-sample updates to reconstruct 59% to 79% of fine-tuning samples with high semantic fidelity.

title: Phoenix: Safe GitHub Issue Resolution via Multi-Agent LLMs
blurb: Phoenix is a multi-agent LLM system that resolves GitHub issues end to end, splitting triage through pull-request creation across six specialized agents coordinated by a label-based webhook state machine with seven layered safety controls. On a 24-instance SWE-bench Lite slice it oracle-resolves 75% of instances with no pass-to-pass regressions, and a pilot on 42 real issues across 14 repositories preserved correctness on every change.

title: AMD acquires MEXT to add predictive memory optimization to its AI stack
blurb: AMD acquired memory optimization startup MEXT, adding predictive software that makes flash storage behave more like DRAM to its AI infrastructure portfolio. The technology uses AI to move frequently accessed data between flash and DRAM, expanding effective memory capacity as enterprises face DRAM supply growth below 16% and a forecasted 130% jump in memory prices by the end of 2026.

title: Query-aware Routing for Filtered Approximate Nearest Neighbors Search
blurb: This paper benchmarks every major categorical filtered ANN method, which combines vector similarity with attribute predicates, and finds no single approach wins across datasets, predicates, or even individual queries. The authors propose a query-aware router that uses a lightweight regression model over three features to predict each method's recall, then picks the best recall-QPS trade-off, beating existing baselines on five unseen datasets with negligible latency overhead.

title: StaminaBench: Stress-Testing Coding Agents over 100 Interaction Turns
blurb: StaminaBench is a benchmark that measures how many consecutive change requests a coding agent can handle before failing, with agents building and modifying a REST API server across 100 procedurally generated turns and codebases up to 6,000 lines. Across six harnesses paired with seven open-source LLMs, all models broke within 5-6 turns, though feeding test results back for retries raised passed-turn counts by up to 12x.

title: The US says ASML’s top chip tool may be in China. ASML says it isn’t
blurb: US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick has told ASML executives he is concerned that one of the company's extreme ultraviolet lithography machines, the only tools capable of printing the most advanced chips, may have reached China in breach of export controls. ASML denies any such system exists in China, and administration officials reportedly cited evidence of EUV-related shipments but declined to show it.

title: AI inference startup Baseten reportedly raising $1.5B months after its last mega-round
blurb: Baseten is reportedly closing a $1.5B round at a $13B valuation, a 160% jump from the $5B valuation it raised at just five months ago, per the Wall Street Journal. The deal is split-priced, with some investors entering at $13B and others at $11B, and is said to be co-led by Spark Capital, Sands Capital, Altimeter Capital, and Wellington Management.

title: US energy regulator to order grid operators to expedite AI data center applications — says projects should bring their own power or cut usage during high demand
blurb: The U.S. Federal Energy Regulatory Commission is preparing an order requiring grid operators to fast-track AI data center connections, prioritizing projects that bring their own power or curtail demand during peak stress. FERC gave grids 90 days to complete the supporting studies, aligning with Trump's AI Action Plan as operators like PJM Interconnection have already raised power costs 75.5%, largely blamed on data center demand.

title: Efficient and Sound Probabilistic Verification for AI Agents
blurb: This paper introduces a framework for verifying security policies on AI agents when predicates are probabilistic, such as a PII detector that fails on some fraction of invocations. Using distributionally robust optimization, it computes sound upper bounds on the probability of policy violation without assuming independence between predicates, outperforming prior work on terminal and tool-calling agent benchmarks while improving the security-utility trade-off.

title: AI demands more engineering discipline. Not less
blurb: The author argues that AI's ability to generate competent code raises the bar for engineering discipline rather than lowering it, drawing a parallel to the shift from handcrafted servers to immutable infrastructure. The piece dates the turning point to Opus 4.5 last November, which it frames as a tipping point rather than the cause, with agentic harnesses, tool use, and MCPs building usability throughout 2025. The takeaway is that code review and rigor matter more as generation gets cheaper, not less.

title: Agentic Resource Discovery: Let agents search
blurb: Agentic Resource Discovery (ARD) is an open specification that lets agents search federated registries for tools, skills, and agents at runtime instead of hardcoding MCP server URLs or dumping every tool description into context. The spec defines an `ai-catalog.json` manifest and a `POST /search` endpoint for intent-based queries, and Hugging Face's Discover Tool wraps the Hub's semantic search to make thousands of running Spaces searchable as skills or MCP servers.

title: AWS unveils agent security, data access tools
blurb: AWS released new security and data access tools for AI agents, including capabilities to surface vulnerabilities in agent code faster and govern how agents reach enterprise data. The updates build on Anthropic's Mythos model and reflect AWS's push to make agentic deployments safer as customers move them into production environments.

title: Analyzing Defensive Misdirection Against Model-Guided Automated Attacks on Agentic AI Systems
blurb: This paper analyzes defensive misdirection as a counter to automated jailbreak and prompt-injection attacks on agentic AI systems, showing that conventional detect-and-block defenses let attacker success rate approach one as query budgets grow because predictable refusals feed the attacker's automated judge. Its proposed method, Contextual Misdirection via Progressive Engagement, replaces refusals with safe but misleading responses and cuts estimated success-rate upper bounds by up to two orders of magnitude on PAIR and GPTFuzz runs.

title: What’s new in Genie Code at Data + AI Summit 2026
blurb: Databricks expanded Genie Code at Data + AI Summit 2026 with a full-page command center for complex, agentic data and ML work, alongside upgrades for production engineering and scheduled tasks. The redesign lets teams run multiple concurrent Genie Code threads across notebooks, SQL, Lakeflow pipelines, jobs, and Unity Catalog assets, tracking execution state and returning to each as results arrive.

title: Automating SKILL.md Generation for Computer-Using Agents via Interaction Trajectory Mining
blurb: This paper introduces a three-stage pipeline that segments GUI interaction trajectories, clusters them into candidate skills, and trains a skill-aware policy from the resulting SKILL.md annotations. The mined clusters are readable, with five of eight hitting at least 0.95 purity against InteraSkill Workflows labels, but GRPO training lifts skill-step accuracy only from 18.5% to 20.5% and underperforms frequency priors, so the authors present it as a diagnostic study rather than a working method.

title: Anthropic ships major Claude Design overhaul with design system imports, code round-trips, and a fix for its token-burning problem
blurb: Anthropic shipped a major overhaul of Claude Design that adds design system imports from GitHub repos and design files, code round-trips, and a fix for the tool's heavy token consumption. Once a design system is imported, Claude builds with those components, checks its output against the system, and auto-corrects to keep generated work on brand and connected to existing enterprise tools.

title: Your Mouse and Eyes Secretly Leak Your Preference: LLM Alignment using Implicit Feedback from Users
blurb: This paper introduces IFLLM, a dataset of mouse trajectories and webcam eye-gaze points from 59 Mechanical Turk workers answering 1,336 multi-turn questions, to align LLMs using implicit feedback rather than explicit ratings. A reward model trained on these signals lifts text-based reward accuracy from 55% to 64% and nearly triples relative response-quality gains after applying DPO across eight LLMs.

title: Beyond Static Endpoints: Tool Programs as an Interface for Flexible Agentic Web Services
blurb: ToolPro represents an agent's tool intent as an executable program that encodes multi-step service interactions with loops, conditionals, and retries, rather than relying on static endpoints. Built over MCP-style services with WebAssembly sandboxing, it uses effect-aware replay for exactly-once state-modifying calls and cuts end-to-end latency by up to 53.4% and client-side traffic by up to 96.1%.

title: AI coding agents may be getting bad instructions from ‘smelly’ config files
blurb: Researchers at Brazil's Federal University of Minas Gerais published the first catalog of "configuration smells" for coding agent files like Agents.md and Claude.md, identifying six recurring flaws including lint leakage, context bloat, skill leakage, and conflicting instructions. The smells appeared in 91 of 100 popular open-source repositories, bloating context and wasting tokens in ways that can make agents like Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor less reliable and more expensive to run.

title: Amazon hopes to challenge Nvidia more directly by selling its AI chips
blurb: Amazon's AI chief Peter DeSantis told Bloomberg that AWS is in early talks to sell its Trainium AI chips to other companies for use in their own data centers, marking a more direct challenge to Nvidia. CEO Andy Jassy has said the chip unit would run at roughly $50B annually as a standalone business, against Nvidia's $326B revenue run rate.

title: FreeStyle: Free Control of Style-Content Dual-Reference Generation from Community LoRA Mining
blurb: FreeStyle is a framework for style-content dual-reference image generation that mines community LoRAs as compositional anchors to build large-scale content-style triplets, addressing the field's shortage of clean training data. It uses a two-stage curriculum to suppress semantic leakage, with an attention-level enrichment constraint for the style-transfer stage and a frequency-aware RoPE modulation strategy for the harder dual-reference stage.

title: From RAG to ontology: Databricks bets on context as the key to trusted AI agents
blurb: Databricks introduced Genie Ontology, a preview context layer that automatically extracts business definitions from enterprise data, dashboards, queries, and documents into a living graph that AI agents can query. It uses a PageRank-inspired ranking to weigh who created a definition, how widely it is used, and how recently it was updated, surfacing the most authoritative answer for each agent.

title: 'A signal of where power sits': Trump and world leaders joined by OpenAI, Anthropic, Google at G7
blurb: The CEOs of OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, and roughly a dozen other AI firms joined Trump and G7 leaders at the summit in Evian to discuss frontier risks, infrastructure, and sovereignty. The gathering follows US export controls on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, which a CFR fellow said signals a shift in who holds power as heads of state increasingly need private-sector endorsement to make credible AI commitments.

title: US pulls the 'kill-switch' on Anthropic's Fable 5 AI models, sending global allies scrambling — European and Canadian leaders alarm allies over sudden export bans
blurb: The Trump administration blocked exports of Anthropic's Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models, cutting off allied governments from direct access to the frontier systems. European and Canadian leaders warned that the sudden restrictions may force them to build their own national AI alternatives to reduce dependence on US-controlled models.

title: Calibration Without Comprehension: Diagnosing the Limits of Fine-Tuning LLMs for Vulnerability Detection in Systems Software
blurb: This paper introduces CWE-Trace, a framework for evaluating LLM vulnerability detection built from 834 manually curated Linux kernel samples spanning 74 CWEs across eight vanilla models and 15 LoRA fine-tuned variants. The authors find that fine-tuning shifts output thresholds without changing decision policy, with the best detection reaching only 52.1% and exact CWE ranking below 1.3% Top-1 accuracy, indicating current LLMs lack reliable security reasoning regardless of strategy.

title: Direct Advantage Estimation for Scalable and Sample-efficient Deep Reinforcement Learning
blurb: This paper extends Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE), a sample-efficient deep reinforcement learning method, to partially observable domains and reduces its computational cost using discrete latent dynamics models that approximate transition probabilities. Tested on the Arcade Learning Environment, the approach scales with function approximator capacity while retaining high sample efficiency.

title: Contagion Networks: Evaluator Bias Propagation in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
blurb: This paper introduces Contagion Networks, a framework for measuring how an LLM evaluator's systematic biases spread across agents in multi-agent systems. In a 3-agent DeepSeek-chat experiment, the authors find biases propagate even within the same model (gamma in 0.157–0.352), identify three regimes governed by spectral radius, and show that expanding the evaluator committee from one to three cuts effective contagion by 72.4%.

title: HydraHead: From Head-Level Functional Heterogeneity to Specialized Attention Hybridization
blurb: HydraHead is a hybrid attention architecture that fuses full attention and linear attention along the head axis rather than the more common layer-wise split, using interpretability analysis to preserve full attention only for retrieval-critical heads. Trained on just 15B tokens, it improves over the baseline by more than 69% at 512K context length and approaches Qwen3.5 while matching a 3:1 layer-wise hybrid's long-context performance at a 7:1 linear-to-full ratio.

title: Source-Grounded Data Generation for Text-to-JSON Learning
blurb: This paper introduces STAGE, a source-grounded pipeline that generates text-to-JSON training data by using LLMs to synthesize reports and schemas while validating ground-truth values against an underlying spreadsheet. On the 851-example STAGE-Eval benchmark, STAGE-generated data raised Qwen3-4B exact match from 31.37% to 74.27% and value accuracy from 45.46% to 90.69%.

title: Multi-LCB: Extending LiveCodeBench to Multiple Programming Languages
blurb: Multi-LCB extends LiveCodeBench from Python-only to twelve programming languages, transforming the original tasks into equivalent problems in each language while preserving LCB's contamination controls and evaluation protocol. Across 24 instruction and reasoning models, the benchmark found evidence of Python overfitting, language-specific contamination, and large disparities in multilingual performance.

title: How to design AI agent loops: schedules, goals, and subagents in Claude Code and Codex
blurb: This podcast breaks down four ways to automate an LLM prompt into a loop: heartbeat, cron, hook, and goal-based, along with the five components any loop needs before production: work trees, skills, plugins, subagents, and state tracking. The author builds two live examples, a daily aging-PR reviewer in Claude Code that schedules itself and spawns subagents, and a weekly skills-identification loop in Codex that uses goal-based subagents to validate its own output.

title: Heterogeneous LLM Debate Under Adversarial Peers: Honest Gains, Replacement Costs, and Resilience
blurb: This paper measures how a heterogeneous peer reshapes honest agents' revision behavior in multi-LLM debate, testing four model families across three reasoning benchmarks. For Llama-3.1-70B defenders on MATH-hard, an honest peer cut the harmful-revision rate from 89% to 35% while an adversarial peer pushed it back to 90%, and when a same-family adversary was already present, adding an honest peer dropped the flip rate on initially-correct items from 31% to 6%.

title: Analyzing the Narration Gap in LLM-Solver Loops
blurb: This paper studies the "narration gap" in hybrid pipelines that pair language models with SAT and SMT solvers, the step where a solver's formally verified output gets turned into a user-facing answer. Evaluating five open-source models under prompt injection, the authors find that certificate gating keeps the solver verdict sound, but an adversary can still invert a verified conclusion during narration, and hardened prompts reduce the attack without eliminating it.

title: [AINews] GLM > GPT? GLM-5.2 passes vibe check; Z.ai forecasts Open Fable by December
blurb: Z.ai's GLM-5.2 has emerged as the first open-weight model that practitioners are treating as a genuine frontier release rather than a benchmaxxed flash in the pan. Artificial Analysis' new knowledge-work benchmark scores it above GPT-5.5, and the broader validation marks Z.ai as a serious frontier lab, with attention now turning to when an open Fable-class model arrives.

title: AI data centers just got a government-mandated fast lane to the grid
blurb: FERC ordered six major grid operators to fast-track interconnection requests from data centers and other large electricity users, with the data centers covering their own connection costs. The unanimous orders open the door to alternative transmission technologies like solid-state transformers but do not address the underlying generating-capacity shortage, even as data center electricity demand is expected to nearly triple through 2035.

title: Amazon has lagged OpenAI and Anthropic, but AI chief sees path to catch up in 'coming year'
blurb: Amazon AI executive Peter DeSantis told CNBC the company aims to compete with OpenAI and Anthropic on frontier models within the "coming year," acknowledging its models haven't matched the leading labs on the most demanding workloads. Amazon now has about 50,000 customers for Nova2, its latest model released in December, and is leaning on custom Trainium and Graviton chips to close the gap.

title: When Global Gating Is Enough: Admission-Time Hubness Control in Anisotropic Vector Retrieval Systems
blurb: This paper studies admission-time defenses against vector hubness in RAG systems, where a single injected document can become the nearest neighbor for many unrelated queries and poison retrieval. Scoring each candidate against sentinel queries before insertion, a global gate hit recall 1.0 at the decisive embedding point and 0.91 on HotFlip attacks with 1% false positives, while adding only 3.1% ingestion latency on HNSW.

title: Sources: about 200 companies accessing Mythos Preview through Project Glasswing have preserved their access despite a recent US government shutdown order (Bloomberg)
blurb: About 200 companies accessing Mythos Preview through Project Glasswing have retained their access despite a recent US government shutdown order, according to sources. The continued access suggests the order has not yet disrupted the preview program, leaving the roughly 200 participating companies operating under their existing terms for now.

title: Microsoft launches Copilot Cowork globally for Microsoft 365
blurb: Microsoft made Copilot Cowork generally available worldwide for Microsoft 365 Copilot customers, following a three-month preview that drew adoption from over half the Fortune 500. The cloud-hosted agentic system executes long-running, multi-tool tasks end-to-end and offers a multi-model architecture including Anthropic Opus 4.8, Sonnet 4.6, GPT 5.5, and the upcoming lower-cost Cowork 1 model, billed via usage-based Copilot Credits.

title: Sources: Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick questioned ASML leaders on concerns China acquired one of its EUV machines, violating US-led export restrictions (Bloomberg)
blurb: US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick questioned ASML leaders over concerns that China obtained one of the company's extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography machines in violation of US-led export controls. EUV systems are the most advanced chipmaking tools and have been blocked from sale to China, making any confirmed transfer a significant breach of the restrictions designed to slow its semiconductor progress.

title: Server-Side Tools Are Now Available for DigitalOcean Inference Engine
blurb: DigitalOcean released Server-Side Tools in Public Preview for its Inference Engine, letting a model call web search, web fetch, Knowledge Bases, MCP servers, and supported Anthropic and OpenAI tools from inside a single inference request. The tools work with an existing Model Access Key, with no separate tool infrastructure or orchestration layer to operate, and web search and fetch are powered by Exa under usage-based pricing.

title: The $13 Billion AI Startup Betting on Cheaper Alternatives to OpenAI, Anthropic
blurb: Baseten is raising $1.5B in a new round, which would value the AI infrastructure startup at $13B. The company sells services that help customers run lower-cost open and alternative models rather than defaulting to OpenAI and Anthropic, positioning it within a growing Silicon Valley ecosystem built around cheaper inference.

title: China tightens indium phosphide checks as AI demand climbs
blurb: China has tightened export scrutiny of indium phosphide, the optical-chip compound used in the transceivers that wire together accelerators inside AI data centers. Six-inch InP wafer prices have climbed roughly 250% to about $5,000 since controls began in early 2025, and the license delays slow data-center buildouts rather than stopping any single chip from working.

title: Databricks lets a Genie One AI agent coworker out of its magic lamp
blurb: Databricks launched Genie One, an agentic coworker in its new Genie suite that connects business users to data inside Databricks and more than 50 external apps including Google Drive, Jira, Slack, and Confluence. The suite runs on Genie Ontology, a self-improving context layer that builds a knowledge graph of company data and ranks assets with an OntoRank method modeled on Google PageRank.

title: LedgerAgent: Structured State for Policy-Adherent Tool-Calling Agents
blurb: LedgerAgent is an inference-time method that maintains a tool-calling agent's task state in a separate ledger rather than leaving it implicit in the prompt, then uses that ledger to block policy-violating tool calls before they execute. Across four customer-service domains and a mix of open- and closed-weight models, it improves average pass^k over standard prompt-based tool-calling, with the largest gains on stricter multi-trial consistency metrics.
