Conduct maps which custom modules a change touches, generates implementation plans and code, and automates the fit-gap analysis that makes years-long SAP S/4HANA migrations so slow, betting that AI modernization is where enterprise budgets move next.

title: What Link data tells us about AI spending
blurb: Stripe analyzed spending across the 250 million customers paying with Link and found AI spend is accelerating sharply. The top 10% of AI spenders nearly doubled their monthly outlay from $183 in December 2025 to $359 in March 2026, a jump that took just three months after the prior climb from $84 to $183 took 22. The clearest signal is where the money goes: that same cohort now spends 5x more on app-builder platforms like Replit, Lovable, and Bolt than it did in January 2025.

title: The 11 standout startups from YC’s Demo Day, according to VCs
blurb: YC's Spring 2026 cohort pushed Demo Day valuations to new highs, with at least two startups fetching $175M or more and investors paying a clear premium for proven, repeat founders. The buzziest names skewed toward defense tech, robotics, AI infrastructure, and agents, led by counter-drone startup 9 Mothers, valued at upward of $200M after booking $1.6M in sales and a contract pipeline it pitches reaching $1B. The signal for founders is that capital is concentrating early around hard-tech and infrastructure bets with visible demand, not consumer apps.

title: Investors Anticipate Fresh Liquidity Following Historic SpaceX Debut
blurb: SpaceX's public debut lands as a queue of multibillion-dollar AI companies prepares to follow it out, and late-stage investors are reading it as the start of a broad distribution event. The IPO unlocks cash that private markets badly need, returning liquidity to funds and LPs that have been sitting on paper gains with no way to realize them. If the SpaceX listing prices well, it sets the reference point every AI company behind it will be measured against.

title: Former Palantir employees raise $60M for AI enterprise startup Conduct
blurb: Three former Palantir employees raised $60M in Series A funding for Conduct, a London startup that makes opaque legacy ERP systems legible to both humans and AI agents. The round was co-led by Index Ventures and Iconiq with a strategic investment from SAP, whose own ERP software Conduct's tools analyze and help companies migrate. The bet is that agents can only act on systems they understand, and decades of customization have made most enterprise ERP incomprehensible even to the people running it, so the unglamorous work of making those systems readable becomes the gate on every AI deployment.

title: Mistral CEO pitches open source AI days after Anthropic ban: ‘We exist outside of state control’
blurb: Mistral is moving to fill the gap left after the US government ordered Anthropic to cut foreign nationals off from two of its best models, with CEO Arthur Mensch pitching open-source AI that "exists outside of state control." The structural argument is sovereignty: open weights let European states and companies inspect, customize, and run models on their own infrastructure, while closed US providers can have access revoked by Washington overnight. Mistral is still the small player, with €3.5bn raised and a reported €20bn round in talks against Anthropic's $965bn, but access risk is now its sharpest selling point.

title: CuspAI to hit $2.6bn valuation with backing from Jeff Bezos, reports say
blurb: CuspAI is raising $400m at a $2.6bn valuation, with Jeff Bezos's family office and Kleiner Perkins joining a round that more than quadruples the $100m mark it set last September. The Cambridge startup runs what amounts to a search engine for materials, with one set of AI models proposing new molecules for uses like sustainable energy and AI compute and another set predicting how they'd perform in the real world. Investors are paying up for AI applied to a hard science problem, not another software wrapper, and a board with Yann LeCun and Geoffrey Hinton is part of what they're buying.

title: French battlefield software startup Comand AI raises €32m from Blossom Capital, Saab
blurb: Comand AI raised €32m in Series A funding led by Blossom Capital, with Swedish defence prime Saab and Polish investor Expeditions joining. The Paris startup builds an AI platform that helps military officers plan, run, and analyze operations, with a stated goal of conducting them four times faster with four times fewer people. The platform is already deployed with units in France, Germany, and Ukraine, and the round funds expansion across NATO markets and into air and maritime domains, as European defence software draws sharper investor attention.

title: Sixty percent of US consumers say 'AI' in brand messaging is a turnoff
blurb: Brands have spent two years and real budget chasing AI brand visibility, yet 61% of US consumers can't name a single company doing it well and 60% say "AI" in brand messaging is a turnoff rather than a feature. The deeper problem is trust: 74% say the web feels less human than a decade ago, and bot fatigue sets in after about 40 minutes. The category has no incumbent and no template, so the first brand to feed AI engines clean data while still giving humans a reason to stay gets to define the standard.

title: Agentic marketing AI startup Gradial grabs $65M in fresh funding
blurb: Gradial raised $65M in a Series C led by Insight Partners, valuing the Seattle marketing-AI startup at $675M and bringing its 16-month total to $110M. While most software companies bolt agents onto their existing products, Gradial is betting on the gaps between tools, running a single layer of agents across Salesforce, ServiceNow, Databricks, and Adobe to author, brand-check, and publish marketing content through a company's existing approval flows. The bet is on orchestration over point tools, a wave of startups now competing to be the connective layer for enterprise agentic deployments.

title: SpaceX falls more than 6% as the record IPO rally cools
blurb: SpaceX fell more than 6% to $178.50 on Thursday, cooling the rally that briefly carried the largest IPO in history past Amazon and Microsoft to a $2.52 trillion market cap. Analysts read the slide as ordinary profit-taking rather than a verdict, and the stock still sits more than 30% above its $135 offering price. The harder question is what justifies the number: Starlink is the only consistently profitable division, while an expensive AI push and a Starship program that just watched its V3 booster explode sit on the cost side of the ledger.

title: Meta lobbies Congress for protection from child-harm lawsuits
blurb: Meta is lobbying Congress for legal immunity from the child-harm lawsuits its products face, reportedly trying to fold a liability shield into the Kids Online Safety Act. The company is contending with more than 2,000 active suits from families, school districts, and state attorneys general, and it has already lost bellwether trials to juries in New Mexico and elsewhere. The remedies those cases seek, from age verification to redesigned recommendation algorithms, reach into how the products work, so a single federal provision that forecloses the claims is cheaper than fighting open-ended exposure case by case.

title: Allbirds continues AI pivot with name change and CEO hire, sending stock soaring
blurb: Allbirds is changing its name to Smartbird and naming Nadia Carlsten, formerly head of AWS's quantum computing center and CEO of Nvidia-partnered DCAI, as its new chief executive, two months after pivoting from shoes to AI infrastructure. Shares jumped 39% on the news, extending a run that sevenfolded the market cap after April's rebrand. The pattern echoes the crypto-mania pivots of the last cycle, and a few of those worked: CoreWeave started as a crypto miner. The market is rewarding the AI label fast, before any compute revenue exists to back it.

title: A US circuit court overturned an injunction against Ohio, allowing the state to implement a law forcing platforms to seek parental consent if a user is under-16 (Nate Raymond/Reuters)
blurb: A US appeals court overturned the injunction blocking Ohio's under-16 social media law, clearing the state to require parental consent before minors can use covered platforms. The ruling weakens the First Amendment shield that has stalled similar age-verification laws in other states, making the patchwork of conflicting rules harder for any consumer platform to ignore. If you run a product that touches users under 16, age-gating and consent flows are shifting from a compliance edge case to table stakes, state by state.

title: Trump Says Anthropic Negotiations Continue as AI Leaders Huddle at G-7
blurb: Trump told reporters at the G-7 summit that talks with Anthropic are "going fine," even as the model shutdown that triggered them drags into its second week. The friction matters because some world leaders showed up worried about losing access to frontier AI tools their own governments and companies now depend on. For founders, the signal is that access to leading models has become a geopolitical variable, not a settled utility, and a single negotiation can throttle the infrastructure your product sits on.

title: Apple Reveals Plans to Raise Prices
blurb: Apple revealed plans to raise prices on its next iPhone lineup, breaking from years of holding the entry tier flat. The move lands as tariffs and higher component costs squeeze hardware margins across the industry, and Apple is choosing to pass that cost to buyers rather than absorb it. For founders selling physical or premium-priced products, it signals that the market leader thinks pricing power still holds, and that customers will tolerate increases when the brand and switching costs are strong enough.

title: Meta Strikes New AI Computing Deals With Data Center Firm Crusoe
blurb: Meta signed new deals to buy AI computing capacity from data center developer Crusoe at two sites in Childress, Texas, and Warrenton, Missouri. The hyperscalers are increasingly renting infrastructure from independent developers rather than building every facility themselves, because the constraint on AI expansion is now power and physical buildout, not capital. Crusoe and its peers are becoming the picks-and-shovels layer underneath the labs, and the contracts are large enough to anchor a developer's entire business before a single rack goes live.

title: How Attention.com Turns Sales Calls Into Pipeline: The Best GTM Data You Own, and Why Most B2B Teams Throw It Away
blurb: Your next quarter's pipeline is already sitting in the sales conversations you had this quarter, and most B2B teams never mine it. Attention.com's playbook runs an agent monthly over recent closed-won deals, extracts buyer function, tenure, company size, and firmographics, then feeds those personas into an enrichment tool to build a dozen specific buyer pockets instead of one broad ICP. The shift that matters is cadence: an annual ICP exercise is a 2021 habit, and the teams compounding fastest refresh it off live closed-won data every month or quarter.

title: Convey closes on $38M round to help companies automate repetitive work with AI teammates
blurb: Convey raised $38M in a Series A led by Andreessen Horowitz, with Khosla Ventures and Pear VC, to let nontechnical workers build and run autonomous AI teammates with no code. The pitch is a bet against the copilot model, which lifts productivity at the margins but leaves the mundane back-office work (pulling spreadsheet data, processing invoices, reconciling financials) on the employee. With global AI spend projected past $2.5 trillion this year and most enterprises still seeing little back-office payoff, the wager is that agents that own a task outright, deployable in about three hours, are what actually moves the number.

title: President Trump Announces Apple and Intel Chip Collaboration | Bloomberg Tech 6/18/2026
blurb: Trump announced that Apple and Intel will partner to produce chips domestically, sending Intel shares higher. The deal pulls Apple's silicon, long fabricated abroad at TSMC, toward US foundries and gives Intel a marquee customer for its struggling contract-manufacturing business. For founders building hardware or anything dependent on chip supply, it signals that domestic fabrication is becoming a policy-backed bet, which could reshape lead times, costs, and sourcing leverage over the next few years.

title: Accenture Takes a Hit on Worsening Outlook and Cloudy AI Future
blurb: Accenture shares fell 18% after the consulting giant said it will book less revenue than expected in the coming months, with management pointing to a softer outlook and uncertainty about how AI reshapes IT services. The selloff is a warning for any business whose model rests on selling human implementation hours. As clients spend on AI tools that compress the work, the firms that resold large teams of consultants are the ones most exposed to the squeeze.

title: Prem seeks $100M Series A as export bans boost sovereign AI demand
blurb: Swiss startup Prem is raising $100M at a valuation of at least $500M to sell software that runs and fine-tunes AI models entirely on a customer's own infrastructure, with data that never leaves. The pitch landed at the right moment: after the US ordered Anthropic to block foreign access to its newest models on June 12, and JPMorgan and Goldman cut Claude access in Greater China, a frontier model stopped being a tool for regulated firms and became a dependency someone else controls. Gartner sees roughly $80B in sovereign cloud spend this year, with European budgets projected to more than triple by 2027.

title: Why we're bullish on loops
blurb: PostHog argues you should stop prompting agents and instead build self-prompting loops with a goal, context, evaluation, and an agent.

title: Game-clip AI startup General Intuition in talks to raise $300M at $2B valuation
blurb: General Intuition is in talks to raise about $300M at a valuation just over $2B, four times the $134M seed it took eight months ago. The startup trains embodied AI agents on roughly 2 billion first-person game clips a year from Medal's 10M-plus monthly users, betting that a player's own view teaches a machine to read and act in physical space better than spectator footage. The data is the moat here, not the model. OpenAI reportedly offered $500M for Medal in 2024 and was turned down, and the world-model field has already pulled in billions this year.

title: PayPal Ventures shutters as company restructuring continues
blurb: PayPal is winding down PayPal Ventures, its corporate venture arm since 2016, pausing new investments and hiring Jefferies to explore secondary sales of holdings that include Plaid, Anchorage Digital, and Talos Global. The move is part of CEO Enrique Lores' restructuring push to "recommit to the fundamentals" and become a technology company again, with more cuts expected over the next few years. The risk is losing the front-row seat to fintech innovation that the fund provided, leaving PayPal blind to emerging startups just as rivals keep their venture arms running.

title: Hyundai to buy SoftBank’s last Boston Dynamics stake for $325m
blurb: Hyundai is buying SoftBank's remaining 9.65% stake in Boston Dynamics for $325m, taking the maker of the Atlas humanoid to full ownership four years after it first took control. The timing matters: Atlas just moved from demo to commercial production, with near-term output earmarked for Hyundai and a planned factory capable of 30,000 humanoids a year. Owning the robotics firm outright gives Hyundai a vertically integrated play against Tesla's Optimus and a wave of Chinese rivals, designing, building, and deploying the machines on its own assembly lines without a second shareholder in the room.

title: US court rules Ohio can restrict children’s use of social media
blurb: A US appeals court ruled that Ohio can require social media companies to get parental consent before letting children under 16 onto platforms like Instagram, reviving a law NetChoice had frozen and rejecting the First Amendment argument the industry leans on nationwide. The split 2-1 decision adds to a patchwork of conflicting state rulings, with similar laws in California, Florida, and Georgia all in litigation. For any platform serving minors, the real cost is compliance: a different consent or age-verification regime in every state, each demanding intrusive identity checks or systems teenagers can route around.

title: New €500 million growth vehicle E2D aims to close Europe’s DefenceTech scaling gap
blurb: Earlybird and AVP launched E2D, a €500M Franco-German growth fund targeting around 20 European dual-use and DefenceTech companies at roughly €25M per ticket, with a first close on June 30th. The fund is built to bridge the expansion gap, backing companies moving from validated technology into large-scale deployment rather than seeding new ones. It lands in a year when European defence capital is concentrating around sovereign capabilities in space, surveillance, and industrial resilience, alongside rounds like ICEYE's €450M Series F.

title: 6 Key Signs a VP of Sales Can’t Scale Beyond $5m-$10m ARR
blurb: The VPs who get you to $5M ARR often can't get you past $10M, and the warning signs show up early. The ones who stall stay only loosely organized after $5M, hire weak or lookalike managers under them, and make excuses as the quarterly number scales from a record month to a record week. The clearest tell is when a VP threatens to quit at the mention of a CRO above them, because the leaders who actually scale know that hire is coming and say the same thing to their own reports.

title: Roelof Botha joins SpaceX’s board of directors
blurb: Roelof Botha joined SpaceX's board less than a week after the company's record-setting IPO, taking a seat on the audit committee alongside longtime Musk confidants. The former Sequoia managing partner brings public-company governance experience, but the board he joins has little real authority: Musk controls more than 80% of voting power and every change to the board's makeup. The signal for founders is that a marquee director hire doesn't always mean independent oversight, and in a founder-controlled company the board is there to advise, not to check the CEO.

title: NVIDIA joins $54M bet on startup that gets AI data centres online three years faster
blurb: Verse raised $54M in a Series B led by Bessemer, with NVIDIA also writing a check, to shrink the grid bottleneck choking AI buildouts. The startup uses on-site battery storage to skip interconnection queues that now run five to seven years at major hubs, getting data centers online up to three years sooner without cutting compute. The real signal is NVIDIA folding Verse into its DSX AI Factory reference architecture, putting the software in front of every operator building to NVIDIA's spec as the race for compute becomes a race for power.

title: Indian Software Stocks Tumble After Accenture Warns on Growth
blurb: Indian software stocks slid Friday after Accenture forecast slower revenue growth, joining a global tech selloff in a sector that has already lost nearly 30% of its market value this year. TCS fell as much as 6.5% to its lowest since 2020, while Infosys dropped 8.7%, tracking a 10% overnight slide in its US-listed shares. The warning signals that AI is now eating into the IT services outsourcing model the Indian giants were built on, and the market is repricing that risk.

title: Intercom co-founder backs $11M round to replace freight dispatchers with AI workers
blurb: Cargofy raised an $11M Series A to replace freight dispatchers with autonomous AI agents, backed by Intercom co-founder Des Traynor. The startup already runs at over $10M ARR across 2,000 customers in Europe, the US, and Central Asia, and one early backer exited via the $5M secondary at more than 50 times their initial check. The bet is structural: freight is a $6 trillion industry facing a chronic labor shortage, and Cargofy's pitch is that one dispatcher on its platform manages a fleet ten times larger than normal.

title: Flagright secures $12.5M Series A to scale AI compliance platform
blurb: Flagright raised a $12.5M Series A led by Infinity Ventures to scale its AI operating system for financial crime compliance. The pitch is consolidation: one platform for transaction monitoring, watchlist screening, risk scoring, case management, and investigations, replacing the legacy systems and point solutions most institutions still stitch together. The differentiator is explainable AI, because regulated banks can only deploy models they can audit and defend, and the round goes toward expanding those use cases and pushing into the US market.

title: AVP et Earlybird lancent fonds de 500 millions d'euros dédié à la défense
blurb: AVP and Earlybird are launching a €500M paneuropean defense fund, a deliberate break from the domestic €100M to €150M vehicles that dominate the European market. The French-German pairing was built to give portfolio companies access to Germany's military budget as Berlin moves to make up for decades of underinvestment, with AVP bringing software and AI and Earlybird bringing historical deep tech. The fund targets around 20 companies at roughly €25M per ticket in Series B and beyond, with a first close expected by the end of June.

title: Israeli Startup M&A in H1 2026: Strategic Buyers Are Back
blurb: Israeli startup M&A kept pace in H1 2026 despite 2025's record $84B in exits, with Apple, Nvidia, Cisco, Palo Alto Networks, CrowdStrike, and ServiceNow all acquiring Israeli companies to fill strategic product gaps rather than chase growth stories. The new wrinkle is that Israeli and Israeli-rooted firms are increasingly buying other Israeli startups, a "blue-and-white" pattern that signals a maturing ecosystem. AI is the through-line in most deals, functioning less as a sector than as the acquisition rationale, with buyers paying for capabilities they believe they need to stay competitive.

title: Australia is the world’s fastest-growing venture ecosystem of the past decade
blurb: Australia is now the world's fastest-growing venture ecosystem, growing 13.7 times faster than any other major hub over the past decade and producing more $10B companies per dollar invested than anywhere else. The catch is that early-stage investment has fallen every year since 2021, and founders now raise 41% of their early-stage capital from overseas, double the share in Europe or the US. The headline returns are real, with Australian VC delivering a 24.4% pooled five-year return, but the domestic capital that should be funding the next Canva is barely reaching seed.

title: An Interview with Michael Morton About E-Commerce in the Age of AI
blurb: Michael Morton argues that the bear cases against AI in e-commerce are unfalsifiable, which is exactly what makes them dangerous to plan around. The sharper question is distribution versus referral: whether AI agents become a new channel you optimize for or a layer that disintermediates the merchant relationship entirely. The interview extends the same logic to grocery and autonomous vehicles, where the hard part is operations, not the model.

title: NSO Group’s founder tripled Dream’s valuation to $3B in four months. Here’s the sovereign AI bet behind it
blurb: Dream, the sovereign-AI and cyber-defence startup co-founded by former NSO Group CEO Shalev Hulio, raised $260M at a $3B valuation, nearly tripling from $1B four months ago. The pitch sells governments AI and security infrastructure they own outright, on systems no foreign tech firm can restrict or switch off, and it has locked in close to $300M in government contracts across Europe, the Middle East, and Southeast Asia since launching in late 2024. Sovereign control of the stack is becoming its own funding thesis, and buyers with geopolitical exposure are paying for it.

title: Sources: Chinese autonomous driving company Momenta has reached a ~$9B valuation as it gears up to raise ~$1B in an upcoming Hong Kong IPO (Jiahui Huang/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Chinese autonomous driving company Momenta has hit a roughly $9B valuation as it prepares to raise about $1B in a Hong Kong IPO. The listing signals that capital for self-driving is rotating toward Chinese players who already ship driver-assistance software in mass-market cars, rather than the US robotaxi names that dominated the last cycle. For founders, Hong Kong is re-emerging as a viable exit venue for hard-tech companies, narrowing the gap with a US IPO market that has stayed largely shut to capital-intensive startups.

title: Artisan’s Ava 2.0: What a Fully Autonomous AI BDR Actually Looks Like in Production with CEO Jaspar Carmichael-Jack
blurb: Artisan's pitch for Ava, its autonomous AI BDR, is accountability rather than automation: instead of a taped-together stack of sequencer, enrichment, and copywriting tools where no single piece owns the outcome, the UI rolls every campaign up to one number, the credits you paid per positive reply or booked meeting. The proof points back it up, with SaaStr's own outbound hitting a 3.6% positive response rate across 7,000 emails on Ava 1.0 and a YC-founder campaign coached to read human clearing 4%. The lesson for operators is that buying a result beats buying software you have to assemble yourself.

title: Malicious JetBrains Plugins Steal AI API Keys as Chrome Extensions Capture Chatbot Chats
blurb: Researchers flagged a coordinated campaign on the JetBrains Marketplace where 15 malicious plugins posed as DeepSeek-based AI coding assistants and exfiltrated developers' AI provider API keys to an attacker-controlled server in plaintext, with two plugins claiming over 25,000 downloads each. The plugins worked as advertised since October 2025 and even ran a paid tier that handed users someone else's API key, suggesting the keys themselves were stolen.

title: Datasette Apps: Host custom HTML applications inside Datasette
blurb: Simon Willison released datasette-apps, a plugin that hosts self-contained HTML+JavaScript applications inside a sandboxed iframe on your Datasette instance, where they can run read-only SQL queries against your data and write queries through configured stored queries. The sandbox uses `allow-scripts allow-forms` plus an injected CSP header to block cookie and localStorage access and outbound HTTP requests, preventing a buggy or malicious app from exfiltrating private data.

title: Zero-Touch OAuth for MCP
blurb: The MCP Enterprise-Managed Authorization extension is now stable, letting organizations control MCP server access through their existing identity provider instead of per-user OAuth and repeated consent prompts. Clients exchange an Identity Assertion JWT grant (ID-JAG) obtained during SSO for an access token, giving end-users zero-touch connection on first login, with Anthropic, Microsoft, and Okta among the early adopters.

title: How hacker group TeamPCP exploited the open source trust model and distribution method to compromise and inject malware into over 1,000 software packages (Matt Kapko/CyberScoop)
blurb: The hacker group TeamPCP compromised over 1,000 software packages by abusing the open source ecosystem's implicit trust and distribution model to inject malware. The campaign exploited maintainer credentials and package-manager defaults rather than novel code vulnerabilities, putting the burden on developers to audit dependencies and lock down supply-chain access.

title: I found 10k GitHub repositories distributing Trojan malware
blurb: A developer discovered roughly 10,000 GitHub repositories distributing Trojan malware, each cloning a legitimate project's commits and periodically re-pushing a single readme change that adds a link to a malicious zip archive. GitHub support took about six weeks to remove the repos the author reported, leaving the broader campaign of identically-patterned, non-forked clones largely intact.

title: ShapedPlugin update flow hacked to infect WordPress sites
blurb: Attackers compromised ShapedPlugin's official update channel and pushed backdoored builds of three paid WooCommerce plugins (Product Slider Pro, Real Testimonials Pro, and Smart Post Show Pro) to paying customers between May 21 and June 12. The malware installs a hidden fake plugin that harvests admin credentials, 2FA secrets, wp-config.php keys, and three months of WooCommerce payment data before self-deleting to cover its tracks.

title: N-Version Programming with Coding Agents
blurb: This paper applies N-version programming to AI coding agents, generating 48 implementations of the Knight-Leveson Launch Interceptor spec across different agents, models, and languages, then testing them on a shared oracle with 1,000,000 randomized inputs. Majority voting across three-version units dropped mean failures from 387.44 to 130.99, and 11,844 N-version units showed zero observed failures, though common-mode failures persisted where the specification was ambiguous.

title: RFC 10008: The new HTTP Query Method
blurb: The IETF published RFC 10008, which standardizes the HTTP QUERY method for safe, idempotent requests that carry their input in the request body rather than the URI. QUERY fills the gap between GET and POST, letting large or sensitive query payloads be sent without URI length limits while remaining cacheable and automatically retriable since it makes no partial state changes.

title: A robot is sprinting towards you. Do you want it running on Claude or Grok?
blurb: A developer at OpenRouter dropped eleven LLMs into a 2D battle royale and ran 30 games, finding that Grok 4.1 Fast won 13 matches at $0.97 per win while Claude Sonnet 4.6, which kept trying to form alliances, took second with 5 wins at $26.78 each. The post's takeaway is that standard benchmarks like Artificial Analysis failed to predict winners, and the model with the most kills, GPT 5.4, never topped the leaderboard.

title: Project Valhalla, Explained: How a Decade of Work Arrives in JDK 28
blurb: On June 15, Oracle engineer Lois Foltan confirmed that JEP 401: Value Classes and Objects, the core of Project Valhalla, is targeting JDK 28 in a pull request adding over 197,000 lines across 1,816 files. This deep-dive traces the decade-long effort to make user-defined types "code like a class, work like an int," and cautions that the JDK 28 cut is preview-only, disabled by default, and just the first part of the project.

title: From the Hugging Face Hub to robot hardware with Strands Agents and LeRobot
blurb: Strands Robots is an open source SDK from AWS (Apache 2.0) that exposes LeRobot's robot abstractions, simulation, and policy inference as AgentTools you compose into a single Strands agent. The same agent code records demonstrations to the Hub, runs policies in simulation, deploys to a physical SO-101 with one keyword-argument change, and fans out to a robot fleet over a Zenoh peer mesh, with LeRobot's own CLIs still handling hardware recording and calibration.

title: Z.ai pitches GLM-5.2 for long-running software engineering tasks
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an MIT-licensed open-source coding model with a one million-token context window that the company says trails Claude Opus 4.8 by 1% on the FrontierSWE long-horizon benchmark. It targets repository-scale agentic work with IndexShare, which cuts per-token compute by 2.9x at full context length, plus multi-token prediction changes that lift speculative decoding acceptance by up to 20%.

title: Bringing more agent harnesses and frameworks to Cloudflare, starting with Flue
blurb: Cloudflare added durable execution, dynamic code execution, a durable filesystem, and dynamic workflows to its Agents SDK, exposing those primitives as a base layer for any agent harness or framework. Flue, a new open-source framework from the team behind Astro, is the first to build on it, shipping its 1.0 Beta this week atop the Pi harness.

title: Beyond LoRA: Can you beat the most popular fine-tuning technique?
blurb: This Hugging Face post argues that engineers default to LoRA for parameter-efficient fine-tuning without considering the dozens of alternatives, and walks through the PEFT library's unified API for testing methods like quantized adapters and other low-memory approaches. It covers when PEFT is worth it over prompting or full fine-tuning, plus LoRA's underdiscussed perks: tiny checkpoints, resistance to catastrophic forgetting, and serving many fine-tunes from one base model.

title: UltraQuant: 4-bit KV Caching for Context-Heavy Agents
blurb: UltraQuant compresses the KV cache to 4 bits for long-context, multi-turn agents, combining Walsh-Hadamard rotation, asymmetric K/V treatment, and an FP4 path with FP8 queries, UE8M0 group scales, and scaled-MFMA kernels on AMD CDNA4 GPUs. On a cache-pressured agentic workload it cuts P50 time-to-first-token by 3.47x in late rounds and lifts output throughput 1.63x over the vLLM FP8 KV baseline.

title: We built a persistent agent memory layer on Elasticsearch with 0.89 recall
blurb: Elastic's Search Labs built a persistent long-term memory layer for agents on Elasticsearch, using three indices structured around cognitive-science memory categories, a hybrid recall query with RRF and a cross-encoder reranker, supersession for contradicted facts, decay, and per-user document-level security. On a QA eval over 168 questions, recall@10 averages 0.89 with zero cross-tenant leaks, and the full implementation is on GitHub.

title: The Correctness Illusion in LLM-Generated GPU Kernels
blurb: This paper shows that standard GPU kernel benchmarks like KernelBench, TritonBench, and GEAK certify buggy LLM-generated kernels as correct because they check only fixed shapes, dtypes, and tolerances with allclose. Re-running 24 Triton and CPU kernels under schema-aware seeded fuzzing against an fp64 reference caught all 9 seeded bugs and passed all 15 controls, with identical verdicts across five GPU classes.

title: Migrate from OpenClaw
blurb: The `hermes claw migrate` command imports an existing OpenClaw setup, including legacy Clawdbot and Moltbot directories, into Nous Research's Hermes agent, mapping config keys for personas, memory, skills, MCP servers, and TTS while archiving anything without a Hermes equivalent. The guide details a dry-run preview before any changes, four-source API key resolution under `--migrate-secrets`, and a `--portal` flag that collapses multi-provider setups into one OAuth login covering 300+ models.

title: Is it agentic enough? Benchmarking open models on your own tooling
blurb: Hugging Face introduces a benchmark that measures not just whether a coding agent solves a task with a library like transformers, but how much work it takes, fanning model × revision × task runs across Hugging Face Jobs on identical hardware. The post argues libraries now need agent-friendly design, a clear CLI, extensive structured docs, and self-contained examples, since a clunky API or stale docs sends the agent down a longer, more expensive path.

title: Proactively reduce tech debt autonomously with AWS Transform – continuous modernization (preview)
blurb: AWS Transform – continuous modernization, now in preview, autonomously scans codebases across thousands of repositories against configurable baselines, then prioritizes tech debt findings and opens pull requests that remediate them. The capability targets end-of-life dependencies, deprecated frameworks, and outdated Lambda runtimes continuously rather than app-by-app, and lets platform teams codify their own policies, such as approved libraries or internal coding standards, to enforce across every repo.

title: Getting more from each token: How Copilot improves context handling and model routing
blurb: GitHub Copilot in VS Code now cuts per-turn overhead with prompt caching, which reuses model state for repeated prompt prefixes, and tool search, which loads tool schemas on demand instead of sending every definition every turn. The goal is spending less of each session on recurring context and more on the task, with Auto routing also expanding so Copilot picks the model that fits the work without the developer choosing each time.

title: Apple showcases its new developer AI tools in impressive 90-minute presentation
blurb: Apple published a 90-minute WWDC26 session, "Inside Apple Intelligence and Xcode," that demos the new Xcode 27, Foundation Models, Core AI, and MLX frameworks for developers. The standout segments build a full app with 3D animations and Visual Intelligence from a few prompts, and run the 1-trillion-parameter Kimi 2.6 model locally across four Mac Studios over RDMA-over-Thunderbolt.

title: npm v12’s Biggest Security Change: From Implicit to Explicit Trust
blurb: npm v12 (estimated July 2026) flips package installation from implicit to explicit trust, blocking three high-risk vectors by default: lifecycle scripts (allowScripts), Git-repository installs (--allow-git), and remote-URL installs (--allow-remote). JFrog's research team ties those mechanisms to roughly 53% of malicious npm attacks over the past year, shifting responsibility for installation-time execution from external scanners onto npm itself.

title: AutoPass: Evidence-Guided LLM Agents for Compiler Performance Tuning
blurb: AutoPass is a training-free multi-agent framework that queries LLVM internals to tune compiler passes, beating -O3 by up to 1.117x.

title: Before the Pull Request: Mining Multi-Agent Coordination
blurb: This paper introduces grite, an open-source coordination substrate for autonomous coding agents that stores a signed, append-only event log inside git itself, capturing how concurrent agents claim and collide over shared work before any PR opens. The shared log cuts redundant re-work from 78% to 0% while tripling useful throughput, and its mineable history surfaces failure modes like lock starvation and race-to-close that PR telemetry never reveals.

title: Probe-and-Refine Tuning of Repository Guidance for Coding Agents
blurb: Probe-and-refine tuning iteratively diagnoses and patches a repository's agent guidance file using synthetic bug-fix probes through single-shot LLM calls, with no agent loop during tuning. On SWE-bench Verified, it lifts Qwen3.5-35B's resolve rate to 33.0% from 28.3% for a static knowledge base, with the gain coming from coverage rather than precision: better guidance helps agents reach the correct file while per-patch precision holds near 59%.

title: AI demands more engineering discipline. Not less
blurb: The author argues that now that models like Opus 4.5 generate code on par with the median engineer, the bottleneck shifts from writing code to verifying it, making code review, tests, and immutable-infrastructure habits more important rather than less. They draw the analogy to the move from handcrafted server "pets" to immutable infrastructure, arguing teams that loosen discipline to move faster with AI are repeating a mistake that earned ops a decade of incidents.

title: Agentic Resource Discovery: Let agents search
blurb: Agentic Resource Discovery (ARD) is an open specification, built by contributors from Microsoft, Google, GoDaddy, and Hugging Face, that lets agents search for and load tools, skills, and other agents at runtime instead of having URLs hardcoded into config files. It sits as a discovery layer in front of MCP, Skills, and A2A, defining a static `ai-catalog.json` manifest plus a `POST /search` registry, with Hugging Face's Discover Tool already indexing thousands of Spaces, Skills, and MCP servers.

title: 144 Mastra npm Packages Compromised via Hijacked Contributor Account
blurb: Attackers hijacked a former Mastra contributor's npm account whose scope access was never revoked and mass-published 144 malicious packages across the @mastra/* namespace, each adding a dependency on "easy-day-js," a dayjs clone that runs a cross-platform crypto-stealing RAT via a postinstall hook. Mastra generated SLSA provenance on CI publishes but did not require it, so a standard personal token could still push unattested versions that npm accepted.

title: StaminaBench: Stress-Testing Coding Agents over 100 Interaction Turns
blurb: StaminaBench measures how many consecutive change requests a coding agent handles before breaking, evaluating six harnesses across seven open-source LLMs on a procedurally generated REST API task that grows to 6,000 lines over 100 turns. Every model failed within 5-6 turns, but feeding test results back for retries improved passed-turn count up to 12x, and harness choice swung strong models by up to 6x.

title: Lore – Open source version control system designed for scalability
blurb: Epic Games released Lore, a centralized, content-addressed version control system that models repository state as Merkle trees over an immutable revision chain. It targets binary-heavy repos at scale through deduplication and sparse, on-demand hydration, positioning it against Perforce for game studios rather than Git's distributed model.

title: GitLab 19.1 released
blurb: GitLab 19.1 shipped secret false positive detection with the GitLab Duo Agent Platform as generally available, automatically analyzing each critical and high severity secret finding after a scan and adding an AI assessment, confidence score, and reasoning to the vulnerability report. The release also adds an always-on availability mode that lets administrators force GitLab Duo on across an entire instance or top-level group, blocking project owners from disabling it for centralized AI governance.

title: Phoenix: Safe GitHub Issue Resolution via Multi-Agent LLMs
blurb: Phoenix is a multi-agent LLM system that resolves GitHub issues end to end, splitting the work across six specialized agents (planner, reproducer, coder, tester, failure analyst, PR) behind seven layered safety controls and a baseline-aware test check. On a 24-instance SWE-bench Lite slice it oracle-resolves 75% with no pass-to-pass regressions, though manual review found half the resulting PRs place code at incorrect paths.

title: How to design AI agent loops: schedules, goals, and subagents in Claude Code and Codex
blurb: This podcast and write-up breaks down four ways to automate a prompt into a loop, heartbeat, cron, hook, and goal, and covers the five components any production loop needs: work trees, skills, plugins, subagents, and state tracking. It then walks through two live builds, a daily aging-PR reviewer in Claude Code that self-schedules and spawns subagents, and a weekly skills-identification loop in Codex whose goal-based subagents validate output in real time.

title: AI coding agents may be getting bad instructions from ‘smelly’ config files
blurb: Researchers at Brazil's Federal University of Minas Gerais published the first catalog of "configuration smells" in coding-agent files like Agents.md and Claude.md, identifying six recurring flaws including lint leakage, context bloat, skill leakage, and conflicting instructions. They found at least one smell in 91 of 100 popular open-source repos, with lint leakage in 62% of files and context bloat in 42%, all of which inflate token costs and degrade how agents interpret project conventions.

title: How software development’s speed obsession enabled TeamPCP’s chaos crusade
blurb: The threat actor TeamPCP has injected malicious code into more than 1,000 software packages in under four months, starting with Trivy in February and exploiting the open-source trust model that lets organizations ingest unvetted dependencies. The author argues the attacks are notable for scale rather than novelty, and that AI coding agents installing unvetted packages with no human in the loop have widened an already-known gap in CI/CD pipelines.

title: Git good with Epic Games' new open source VCS, Lore
blurb: Epic Games released Lore, an open source version control system built to handle the massive binary assets and monorepo scale that Git struggles with on large game projects. The system targets workflows where artists and engineers commit multi-gigabyte files, positioning it as an alternative to Perforce for studios that have outgrown Git's large-file handling.

title: Multi-LCB: Extending LiveCodeBench to Multiple Programming Languages
blurb: Multi-LCB extends LiveCodeBench beyond Python to twelve programming languages by transforming existing LCB tasks into equivalent problems while preserving the benchmark's contamination controls and evaluation protocol. Evaluating 24 instruction and reasoning models, the authors found clear Python overfitting, language-specific contamination, and wide gaps in multilingual code-generation performance.

title: Google sets timeline for Android developer verification enforcement
blurb: Google set September 30, 2026 as the enforcement date for Android developer verification, starting with users in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand, where apps distributed through stores like Google Play, Galaxy Store, and GetApps must be linked to a verified identity before installation. New APIs let developers register package names in bulk or via CI/CD, and a fee-free limited distribution account lets hobbyists ship to up to 20 devices without a government ID.

title: Beyond Static Endpoints: Tool Programs as an Interface for Flexible Agentic Web Services
blurb: ToolPro represents an agent's tool intent as an executable program that encodes multi-step service interactions with loops, conditionals, and retries, rather than forcing static endpoint calls. Running over MCP-style services in a WebAssembly sandbox, it uses effect-aware replay for exactly-once state-modifying calls and cuts end-to-end latency by up to 53.4% and client-side traffic by up to 96.1%, with bigger gains under higher network latency.

title: AWS aims to take the pain out of RAG with Bedrock Managed Knowledge Base
blurb: AWS released Bedrock Managed Knowledge Base, a managed RAG service that auto-selects and maintains the embeddings, re-ranker, and foundation models behind enterprise retrieval, plus six native connectors for S3, SharePoint, Confluence, Google Drive, OneDrive, and web content. Smart Parsing and an Agentic Retriever target accuracy across mixed content types and multi-repository queries, with the trade-off being less control over model choice and pipeline internals.

title: ICYMI: ZAI launches GLM-5.2 open model with 1M context
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an open-weight coding model with a 1M-token context window and up to 128K output tokens, built for long-horizon agent work across full repositories. The 744B-parameter model scores 81.0 on Terminal-Bench 2.1 and 62.1 on SWE-bench Pro, uses an IndexShare sparse-attention scheme to cut per-token FLOPs 2.9x at 1M context, and plugs into Claude Code, Cline, and OpenClaw via an OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

title: FAPO: Fully Autonomous Prompt Optimization of Multi-Step LLM Pipelines
blurb: FAPO is a framework that lets Claude Code autonomously optimize multi-step LLM pipelines, evaluating runs, diagnosing failures, and proposing scoped prompt edits before escalating to chain-structure changes only when attribution points to a structural bottleneck. Across six benchmarks and three task models it beats the GEPA baseline in 15 of 18 comparisons, averaging +14.1pp, and rising to +33.8pp on the six cases where prompt-first search escalated to structural rewrites.

title: Anthropic's Claude Code Artifacts update brings live, shared dashboards and interactive workspaces to enterprises
blurb: Anthropic added Artifacts to Claude Code on Team and Enterprise plans, turning a session's work into a live, shareable HTML page that builds from the local repo, connected data sources, and monitoring tools without standing up extra infrastructure. The page refreshes in-place at a fixed URL as the agent works and versions every change for rollback, landing about two weeks after OpenAI shipped a similar enterprise hosting feature called Sites for Codex.

title: Clojure Hosted on Go
blurb: Glojure is an interpreter for Clojure hosted on Go, giving Clojure code direct access to Go libraries the way the JVM version reaches Java. As a hosted language, all Go values work as Glojure values and vice versa, though the project is early (v0.3.0), runs only a subset of core Clojure, and makes no backwards-compatibility guarantees before v1.

title: SteamOS Linux 3.8 released as stable
blurb: Valve promoted SteamOS 3.8 to its stable channel, shipping the Arch-based handheld OS with an updated Linux kernel, a newer Mesa graphics stack, and broader hardware support beyond the Steam Deck. The release continues Valve's push to run SteamOS on third-party handhelds and desktops, giving Linux gaming developers a more current Proton and driver baseline to target.

title: New AI optimization framework beats Claude Code and Codex by 2.5x on the same compute budget
blurb: Researchers at Renmin University of China and Microsoft Research released Arbor, an autonomous optimization framework that organizes hypotheses, experiments, and insights into a tree so the agent learns from prior failures instead of repeating trial-and-error. On real-world engineering tasks under the same compute budget, Arbor delivered more than 2.5x the verifiable performance gains of standard coding agents like Claude Code and Codex.

title: Apple announces major App Store changes for Brazil, including alternative app marketplaces
blurb: Apple opened iOS in Brazil to alternative app marketplaces and outside payment methods for users on iOS 26.5 and up, settling a CADE dispute that started with a 2022 MercadoLibre complaint. Apps distributed off the App Store still go through baseline Notarization and must carry age ratings, and the changes bar direct web sideloading while introducing a Japan-style commission structure.

title: Local Qwen isn't a worse Opus, it's a different tool
blurb: The author argues that local Qwen models aren't a budget Opus substitute but a distinct tool with a different reliability profile, drawing on production use across a bootstrapped software business rather than benchmark claims or airplane-coding anecdotes. An RTX 6000 Pro paid for itself within two or three months, but the post is candid about Qwen's worst failure modes: infinite loops and hallucination that worsen once you quantize down to fit a consumer GPU.

title: Launch HN: Adam (YC W25) – Open-Source AI CAD
blurb: Adam, a YC W25 startup, open-sourced CADAM, a browser-based tool that turns natural-language prompts and images into parametric OpenSCAD models exportable as .STL, .SCAD, or .DXF. It runs entirely client-side via WebAssembly, ships with the BOSL, BOSL2, and MCAD libraries, and includes interactive sliders for adjusting dimensions plus a benchmark suite covering full multi-part assemblies down to single parametric parts.

title: Agentic coding deserves more than a chat box bolted onto VS Code
blurb: Polypore is an open-source agentic desktop IDE built on Tauri 2, where every surface is a dockable sandboxed panel and the agent is the primary actor across the layout, memory system, debug tooling, and a bundled Node MCP sidecar exposing 22+ tools. Secrets stay in the OS keyring and are swapped for handle sentinels before any agent spawns, so the model drives panels and injects credentials over RPC without ever seeing plaintext.

title: Emacs 31 is around the corner: The changes I'm daily driving
blurb: Emacs 31 is in pre-release, and the author has been daily-driving builds from the emacs-31 and master branches for months, folding each new feature into a no-external-packages config. The standout change is built-in tree-sitter support: setting `treesit-enabled-modes` to `t` auto-switches major modes to their tree-sitter variants, while `treesit-auto-install-grammar` fetches and compiles missing grammars on demand, replacing the old manual `treesit-language-source-alist` ceremony.

title: Smelly config files will make your agents waste tokens, researchers warn
blurb: Researchers found that poorly structured configuration files force coding agents to burn extra tokens re-parsing ambiguous or redundant context, with "smelly" configs measurably inflating the per-task cost of agent runs. The takeaway for engineers is to treat agent config like code, pruning dead settings and tightening structure so the model spends its context window on the task instead of decoding your setup.

title: .gitignore Isn't the only way to ignore files in Git
blurb: Git supports three levels of file ignoring beyond the standard checked-in .gitignore: `.git/info/exclude` for per-repo rules that stay out of version control, and `~/.config/git/ignore` for machine-wide patterns like .DS_Store. The post covers configuring a custom global ignore path via `core.excludesFile` and using `git check-ignore -v` to trace which file and line is excluding a given path.

title: Designing frontend systems for cloud latency, not just cloud failure
blurb: The author argues that frontend reliability should account for cloud latency, not just outright failure, since distributed dependencies, serverless cold starts, and asynchronous state propagation make slow responses a normal operating condition rather than an exception. The piece makes the case that interfaces designed around ideal conditions feel broken to users well before anything technically fails, because people rarely distinguish "slow" from "broken.

title: The Siren Song of ariaNotify()
blurb: The new ariaNotify() method, defined in the WAI-ARIA 1.3 spec, lets developers programmatically trigger screen reader narration via a simple call like `document.ariaNotify("Hello, World.")`, replacing the off-label use of ARIA live regions that historically handled asynchronous content changes. The author cautions that it should be reserved for cases that cannot be solved any other way, since live regions remain the standard approach despite their well-known awkwardness.

title: The Bi-Channel Networking Paradigm for Database Systems in the Cloud
blurb: This paper proposes a bi-channel networking paradigm for cloud databases, splitting traffic into a high-performance user-space UDP data path for latency- and bandwidth-sensitive operations and a kernel TCP control path for coordination and recovery. The co-design sidesteps the kernel TCP CPU overhead that bottlenecks fast cloud networks, saturating 200 Gbit/s in a distributed shuffle with three cores while preserving TCP's reliability guarantees.

title: Write cleaner and faster Python code
blurb: InfoWorld's Python roundup covers Meta's Pyrefly 1.0 type-checking linter, which ships with PyTorch and Django support, and Python 3.15's new sampling profiler for low-overhead bottleneck hunting. It also walks through virtual environment tooling and flags Hades, a supply-chain malware strain that hides in PyPI packages and evades LLM-based code analysis.

title: Your browser tab could become encrypted storage for someone else’s files
blurb: Safecloud, a system from IENYC professor Gregory Magarshak, builds decentralized encrypted storage where browser tabs act as "Drop" nodes holding ciphertext chunks in IndexedDB while routing servers hold no keys. Files are split into fixed-size chunks encrypted client-side, with chunk addresses computed over encrypted bytes so storage nodes can verify content without decrypting it, and a single key-derivation hierarchy that also enables one-step segment seeking for streaming media.

title: The Thundering Herd Problem in Agentic AI: Why Traditional Fixes Fall Short
blurb: This CockroachDB post argues that the thundering herd problem changes shape in agentic AI, because the synchronized load is generated internally by intentional fan-out and parallelization rather than by an external trigger you can trace. The author identifies a non-linear saturation point between 700 and 1,000 concurrent agents where behavior shifts sharply, meaning load tests at 500 agents predict almost nothing about 5,000 and most teams hit the wall in production.

title: Angular Signals in practice: Building a signal-first form in Angular
blurb: Angular's Signal Forms API expresses form behavior as state and derivation rather than event orchestration, and this walkthrough builds a registration form (email, password, confirmation, terms acceptance) to show how validation, interaction state, and submission logic change in practice. The author argues much of the coordination logic disappears when forms are declarative, while flagging that teams should weigh the new trade-offs before adopting the approach broadly.

title: I hate compilers
blurb: The author needed reproducible builds of `wasm2js` for Anubis, but identical source produced byte-different binaries across machines, so they vendored a deterministic copy compiled to WebAssembly via wasi-sdk. Getting there meant passing `--no-wasm-opt` to stop Clang silently invoking a system tool during linking, and disabling address-space randomization with `setarch` to kill ~29-byte diffs from pointer-dependent catch-reference ordering, with separate x86_64 and arm64 checksums as the remaining trade-off.

title: Migrating from GNU Stow to Chezmoi
blurb: Developer migrates dotfiles from GNU Stow to chezmoi, swapping symlinks for a managed source repo to keep three Macs converged.

title: CS 6120: Advanced Compilers: The Self-Guided Online Course (2020)
blurb: Cornell's PhD-level Advanced Compilers course, free and self-guided, covering IRs, dataflow, SSA, LLVM, and GC through papers and hacking tasks.

title: AWS enters the context layer race with a graph that learns from agents, not manual curation
blurb: AWS announced a context intelligence stack for AI agents, led by AWS Context, a knowledge graph service that automatically maps relationships across existing data and refines itself based on which sources agents actually use. It publishes all metadata as Apache Iceberg to S3 Tables queryable via Athena, Redshift, or Spark, and ships alongside GA of S3 Annotations and a preview of skill assets in Glue Data Catalog.

title: What’s new in Genie Code at Data + AI Summit 2026
blurb: Databricks expanded Genie Code at Data + AI Summit 2026 with a full-page command center, production data and ML engineering upgrades, and scheduled tasks, letting teams run and track multiple agentic threads across notebooks, SQL, Lakeflow pipelines, and Unity Catalog assets. The redesign targets multi-step development that spans many assets, with Databricks reporting Genie usage grew 10x over the past year and now reaches 90% of its customers.

title: From RAG to ontology: Databricks bets on context as the key to trusted AI agents
blurb: Databricks introduced Genie Ontology, a preview context layer that auto-extracts business definitions from data, dashboards, queries, and docs into a living graph, ranking the most authoritative sources with a PageRank-style system so every agent reads from one definition instead of whatever RAG pulls back. The pitch is consistency and lineage-backed trust, the recurring blocker to enterprise AI adoption. Analysts caution that data quality and governance, not the ontology itself, will decide whether teams actually rely on the answers.

title: Source-Grounded Data Generation for Text-to-JSON Learning
blurb: STAGE is a synthetic-data pipeline for training models to extract structured JSON from long unstructured documents like financial filings and clinical records. It uses LLMs to synthesize reports and JSON schemas at scale while validating every ground-truth value against the underlying spreadsheet, so the training data stays accurate. Fine-tuning Qwen3-4B on STAGE data lifted exact match from 31.37% to 74.27% and value accuracy from 45.46% to 90.69% on the authors' 851-example benchmark.

title: Datasette Apps: Host custom HTML applications inside Datasette
blurb: Datasette Apps, a new plugin, lets you host self-contained HTML+JavaScript applications inside Datasette that run in a sandboxed iframe and query your data with read-only SQL, or write queries when configured with stored queries. The sandbox blocks cookie and localStorage access and injects a CSP header that prevents outbound HTTP requests, so a buggy or malicious app cannot exfiltrate private data.

title: Databricks targets AI operations bottlenecks with ZeroOps
blurb: Databricks released Genie ZeroOps, an agentic operations capability in private preview that automates monitoring, root-cause analysis, and remediation across data and AI workloads, using Unity Catalog lineage to trace anomalies and test proposed fixes in isolation before human review. The pitch targets the maintenance tax that now consumes most data-team time, shifting engineers from firefighting pipeline and model failures to approving AI-generated fixes.

title: Data Intelligence Agents: Interpreting, Modeling, and Querying Enterprise Data via Autonomous Coding Agents
blurb: This paper introduces Data Intelligence Agents (DIA), a production system of three agents (Data Interpreter, Schema Creator, Query Generator) that treats autonomous coding agents as the core abstraction, generating, executing, validating, and repairing real artifacts instead of emitting text. The Query Generator matches or beats the best published results on all seven SQL benchmarks across four task types and four dialects in fully autonomous mode. The authors argue execution-grounded agents with shared memory generalize across the data integration workflow, with adaptation confined to natural-language instructions.

title: Evaluating Learned Spatial Indexes
blurb: This paper benchmarks learned spatial indexes for range queries, isolating how block size, data and query skew, selectivity, and disk-versus-memory storage shift the refinement-versus-scan trade-off that determines latency. The authors run every index on a shared storage backend and standardized query pipeline, then distill the results into a workload-based decision tree for picking an index. Validated on real OpenStreetMap point sets with synthetic queries, the tree showed minimal decision regret and near-optimal performance.

title: Caching for Dollars, Not Hits: An Exact Offline Reference for Cloud-Egress Caching and the Crossover That Decides When It Pays
blurb: This paper reframes cache design around dollars instead of hit rate, arguing that when misses fetch from cloud object storage, the bill is per-GET and per-byte egress, so a rarely fetched but expensive object can cost thousands of times more than a frequent cheap one. The authors build an exact offline dollar-optimum benchmark and show cost-aware GreedyDual cuts LRU's regret to roughly a tenth. A closed-form crossover, GET_fee/egress_rate (about 4KB on S3, 330B on GCS), predicts which deployments actually need dollar-aware caching.

title: Databricks expands Lakehouse to unify OLAP and OLTP
blurb: Databricks introduced LTAP (Lake Transactional/Analytical Processing) at its Data + AI Summit, combining Lakebase serverless Postgres with the Lakehouse under one Unity Catalog governance model so operational data is queryable for analytics with no pipelines. LTAP ends the separate copying between transactional and analytical stores, keeping data in open Iceberg and Delta formats while letting OLTP and OLAP workloads scale independently with strict isolation.

title: Query-aware Routing for Filtered Approximate Nearest Neighbors Search
blurb: Filtered ANN search pairs vector similarity with attribute predicates, and this paper benchmarks every major categorical method across datasets and three predicate types, finding no single winner even within one dataset. The authors propose query-aware routing: a lightweight model predicts each method's recall per query, then a router consults an offline recall/QPS table to pick the best trade-off. Trained on six datasets and tested on five unseen ones, it leads existing baselines with negligible latency overhead.

title: Databricks lets a Genie One AI agent coworker out of its magic lamp
blurb: Databricks launched Genie One, an agentic coworker built on Genie Ontology, a self-improving context layer that builds a knowledge graph across Databricks plus 50-plus connected apps including Slack, Jira, Confluence, and Google Drive. The system ranks enterprise data assets with an OntoRank scheme modeled on PageRank, giving multiple agents a shared set of metric definitions and master data so answers stay consistent across the org.

title: What’s coming next to Free Edition
blurb: Databricks added five practitioner features to its Free Edition: Genie Code for autonomous code generation, serverless GPUs, the Postgres-compatible Lakebase, Agent Bricks for building AI agents, and Lakeflow Designer. The move puts every core Databricks feature in the free tier, extending the funnel that has already handed $10M+ in credits to 500,000+ learners building data engineering, ML, and agent projects.

title: Real-time anomaly detection and log monitoring for dbt core
blurb: The author argues that out-of-the-box dbt logs only alert after a full run compiles, and walks through adding real-time, model-level alerting to dbt Core. The setup runs `dbt build --log-level debug --log-format json` and pipes stdout to a sidecar process that streams structured logs and fires Slack alerts the moment a model fails. The piece pushes past where Elementary and dbt Cloud stop, adding live anomaly detection for models that run slower than normal or trip arbitrary conditions.

title: AgenticDB: Agentic Performance Reconfiguration for Database Workloads
blurb: AgenticDB reframes database tuning as an agentic reconfiguration loop rather than iterative knob search, proposing both DBMS- and OS-level changes, applying them under safety constraints, and using runtime feedback to diagnose bottlenecks and guide later decisions. Tested on MySQL and PostgreSQL across YCSB, Sysbench, and TPC-H, it beat the strongest baseline by 118.1% on average while cutting time-to-best by 22.6%. The gains came from the OS-level action space, a robust execution lifecycle, and memory that carries experience across tasks.

title: Databricks launches AI co-worker, Genie One
blurb: Databricks released Genie One, an AI coworker that queries governed company data via SQL and acts through MCP tools across 50+ connected apps.

title: Policy-aware Vector Search: A Vision for Fine Grained Access Control in Vector Databases
blurb: This paper proposes fine-grained access control for vector databases, formalizing a policy model and enforcement problem for the RAG and organizational AI pipelines that increasingly query embeddings in security-sensitive contexts. The authors show the core tension: enforcing per-user policies correctly while keeping ANN recall high and query latency low, since vector search mixes structured filters with approximate semantic results in ways relational FGAC patterns do not handle. The work compares enforcement strategies and lays out open problems rather than shipping a finished system.

title: Beyond Accuracy: Measuring Logical Compliance of Predictive Models
blurb: This paper introduces the Rule Violation Score (RVS), a metric that measures how well a predictive model respects predefined logical constraints, independent of its accuracy. RVS handles hard and soft rules separately, works on any model over a relational vocabulary, and compiles to auto-generated SQL queries for Horn rules. The authors show that two models with comparable accuracy can diverge sharply in logical compliance, a gap standard metrics miss and a real concern for healthcare, finance, and autonomous systems.

title: DeQL: A Decision Query Language for Prescriptive Analytics over Relational Data
blurb: DeQL extends SQL to express decision queries, adding two constructs: CREATE CANDIDATES, which defines the option space from relational sources, and DECIDE, which declares decision variables, named constraints, and an objective. The user states what to optimize and the engine picks the solver, keeping the structure of subset selection, allocation, scheduling, and assignment problems visible inside the warehouse. This first specification fixes the core constructs and adds extensions for optimization under uncertainty, inline model scoring, and time-bounded solving.

title: The Bi-Channel Networking Paradigm for Database Systems in the Cloud
blurb:
This paper argues database systems should stop treating the network as a black box and co-design it with database operations. The proposed bi-channel paradigm splits communication into a high-performance user-space UDP data path for latency- and bandwidth-sensitive work and a kernel TCP control path for coordination and recovery, exploiting modern NICs without reimplementing reliability. The authors saturate 200 Gbit/s on a distributed shuffle with three CPU cores and push millions of messages per second through a replicated key-value store.

(83 words)

title: Vibe Coding for Visualization Implementation: An Empirical Study of Practices and Challenges
blurb: This paper studies how people actually use AI "vibe coding" tools to build data visualizations, running 16 participants through two viz tasks plus interviews to map their prompting, evaluation, and iteration habits. The finding that matters for data teams: visualization is a worse fit for natural-language generation than general coding, because charts demand precise intent-to-pixel alignment that users struggle to specify and verify, leaving them stuck in iteration loops when the output looks plausible but encodes the data wrong.

title: Anthropic ships major Claude Design overhaul with design system imports, code round-trips, and a fix for its token-burning problem
blurb: Anthropic overhauled Claude Design, adding design-system imports from GitHub repos or design files, code round-trips, and a rebuilt editor that cuts the heavy token consumption that let early users exhaust a weekly Claude Pro allowance in under half an hour. The update repositions the tool from a prototyping demo into a brand-compliance layer that checks generated UI against imported components and auto-corrects before export.

title: Apple confirms price increases are coming to its products due to RAM shortage
blurb: Apple confirmed that price increases across its product line are coming, with CEO Tim Cook telling The Wall Street Journal the hikes are "unavoidable" as DRAM and storage costs surge and AI-server demand for high-bandwidth memory squeezes consumer supply. Macs and iPads could see increases before the September iPhone 18 launch, and the squeeze lands just as Apple's cheapest laptop, the 8GB MacBook Neo, leans on the same constrained memory.

title: Anthill caverns inform "earthy and tactile" Indian home by Kaushal Tatiya Architects
blurb: Kaushal Tatiya Architects modeled The Anthill, a 650-square-metre home in Ahilyanagar, Maharashtra, on the cool earth chambers of an anthill, stacking curved brick balconies across its facade to passively cool the interior. The design keeps living spaces comfortable in the region's hot, dry climate without mechanical ventilation, a useful reference for anyone weighing material and form against energy load.

title: Adobe expands Firefly capabilities, extends agentic tools to Creative Cloud apps
blurb: Adobe expanded Firefly with pre-built Creative Skills, including a Brand Kit creation flow that generates logos, palettes, fonts, and social-channel promo videos from a single prompt, plus new Elements and Projects features for reusing characters and organizing assets. The company also added agentic Firefly sidebars to Premiere, Photoshop, Illustrator, InDesign, and Frame.io, letting users delegate tasks like renaming assets, fact-checking against briefs, and applying brand-guideline updates.

title: Adobe embeds agentic AI workflows across Creative Cloud, shifting from media generation to production orchestration
blurb: Adobe moved its Creative Agent into public beta across Premiere Pro, Photoshop, Illustrator, InDesign, and Frame.io, repositioning its AI from media generator to an orchestration layer that reads natural-language prompts and drives each app's APIs to run multi-step production tasks. The upgraded Firefly studio adds Elements, a visual variables library for reusing characters and objects across generations, and Projects, a persistent memory layer that retains assets and session context.

title: Adobe’s new AI assistant could save you hours in Photoshop and Premiere
blurb: Adobe expanded its creative agent across Firefly and Creative Cloud, embedding AI assistants in Photoshop, Premiere Pro, Illustrator, InDesign, and Frame.io that execute multi-step workflows from a natural-language goal. The bet positions Adobe's agent as a connective layer across the entire production process, alongside new Firefly tools for brand-kit generation, storyboard-to-video, and a redesigned studio with reusable Elements and Projects assets.

title: Affinity Will Now Come Pre-Installed on Windows Surface Computers
blurb: Microsoft will pre-install Canva's Affinity design suite on its new Snapdragon X2 Surface laptops, pinning the free professional photo, design, and publishing app to the Start menu and wiring haptic feedback into the touchpad and Slim Pen for Affinity editing. The deal is a notable distribution win for Affinity, which added three million users in the month after relaunching as a free tool late last year.

title: The Siren Song of  ariaNotify()
blurb: The new ariaNotify() method, defined in the WAI-ARIA 1.3 spec, lets developers programmatically trigger screen reader narration with a single string argument, replacing the brittle workarounds teams previously built on top of ARIA live regions. The author makes the case for treating it as a last resort, reserved for problems that genuinely cannot be solved otherwise, including pushing back on the feature that created the need.

title: A2UI under the hood: Designing for the new era of radically adaptive UI
blurb: A2UI is an open protocol from Google, now shaped by CopilotKit and others, that lets an AI assemble interfaces on the fly from a designer-defined catalogue of components rather than generating raw div-and-Tailwind markup. The author argues this "radically adaptive UI" approach keeps designers in control of the building blocks while the screen reassembles itself around each request, though the spec is young and the design-to-code pipeline does not exist yet.

title: Snap Specs bring rich augmented reality to smart glasses
blurb: Snap released the Specs, standalone AR glasses that combine the immersive display of a mixed-reality headset with the lightweight frame of consumer AI glasses, shown this week at the Augmented World Expo. The product targets a middle ground between bulky passthrough headsets and display-less audio glasses, betting that designers and developers will build for a form factor light enough to wear in public.

title: Unreal Engine 5 games aren’t getting bigger anymore – they’re getting smarter
blurb: Nvidia's ACE Game Agent SDK now sits inside Unreal Engine 5, letting developers build "agentic" NPCs from an Agent API, a Chat API, and a retrieval layer that keeps responses grounded in live game state rather than drifting into generic-assistant mode. Epic's PUBG Ally companion is the first visible test, and the toolkit runs speech, on-device language models, and text-to-speech locally to cut the latency that breaks the illusion.

title: How&How gives Brunel's SS Great Britain a second life as Bristol Dockyards
blurb: How&How rebranded Brunel's SS Great Britain as the Bristol Dockyards, expanding the single ship into a three-part cultural destination spanning the Being Brunel museum, the Brunel Institute archive, and the vessel itself. The identity leans on a collage system that layers 200 years of timelines, typography, and dockyard ephemera, plus a defiantly un-nautical pink pulled from Totterdown's terraced houses to counter slipping ticket sales and an ageing audience.

title: Modos Color Monitor Pushes E-Paper Displays Further
blurb: Modos launched a Crowd Supply campaign for Flow, a 13.3-inch color e-paper monitor at 3,200x2,400 with touch and a 60-Hz refresh.

title: Why did Gaudí not have more of an impact on global architecture?
blurb: Dezeen's Nat Barker argues that Gaudí's outsized fame never translated into architectural influence, because his hand-built, intuitive methods produced forms too singular to systematize or teach. The piece, marking the centenary of his death, is a sharp reminder that visual originality and disciplinary impact are separate currencies.

title: Siri AI will make the Apple Watch a fully-fledged AI wearable in watchOS 27
blurb: Apple will bring its overhauled Siri AI and a dedicated Siri app to the Apple Watch in watchOS 27, extending the Apple Intelligence stack to the wrist for the first time after the device sat out the 2024 rollout. The pitch is an always-on, screen-free assistant available even without an unlocked iPhone or AirPods nearby, and watchOS 27 requires Series 9, Ultra 2, or SE 3 and later.

title: Heatherwick Studio unveils "space observatory" in AlUla
blurb: Heatherwick Studio released designs for AlUla Manara, a textured-stone stargazing and research center planned for the Saudi desert near the AlUla UNESCO World Heritage Site. The building is shaped around the region's exceptional night-sky visibility, pairing a public visitor experience with astronomical research facilities in one of the few remaining low-light-pollution sites accessible to a mass audience.

title: Adobe brings its Firefly AI Assistant inside of Premiere, Photoshop and Illustrator
blurb: Adobe is adding a Firefly AI Assistant sidebar inside Premiere, Photoshop, Illustrator, InDesign, and Frame.io, giving the agent a direct interface within each app rather than only running prompts behind the scenes. A previewed Firefly studio update adds an Elements feature for saving and reusing generated characters, locations, and objects, plus new skills for brand identity, rough cuts, and storyboard-to-video generation.

title: ZHA designs waterfront performance venue in China to "crescendo and flare"
blurb: Zaha Hadid Architects completed the Songshan Lake Exhibition and Performance Center, a waterfront complex of three interconnected volumes whose curved forms reference traditional Cantonese opera costumes. Sited in Guangdong's Greater Bay Area, the building anchors an undulating 1,200-seat Grand Theatre alongside exhibition space, extending ZHA's signature parametric language into a civic cultural venue for south China.

title: VSCO unveils Studio Pro app as it pushes further into professional photography workflows
blurb: VSCO released Studio Pro on iOS, a pro-focused editor that batch edits up to 100 photos at once and includes Style Match, which analyzes any image and rebuilds its color, tone, and mood from a tailored mix of presets. The app slots into VSCO's expanding business stack alongside Workspace, Canvas, and Sites, and anchors the upcoming $499.99/year VSCO One bundle, with a macOS version due later this year.

title: Epic Games says Unreal Engine 6 will help developers "build content faster" using AI models
blurb: Epic Games detailed Unreal Engine 6, which moves gameplay programming to Verse, makes content and economies portable across games via open standards, and bakes in generative AI through an MCP with Claude and Gemini integrations. Early access is targeted for late 2027 with a full release 12 to 18 months later, and the company is shipping Unreal Engine 5.8 as the current branch's final major update.

title: ‘Hard Fork’ Live Part 2: Dylan Field on Standing Out in the A.I. Era
