blurb: Figma CEO Dylan Field argued on a live "Hard Fork" episode that designers and writers with a distinct creative voice should take public risks now, framing the AI era as an opening rather than a threat. His pitch reframes the standout-or-fade anxiety around AI tooling as a case for putting personal work out there, betting that human voice becomes more valuable as generation gets cheaper.

title: VSCO's new Studio Pro app can edit 100 photos at a time
blurb: VSCO released Studio Pro, an iOS editing app whose core feature is batch editing up to 100 photos at once, plus a Style Match tool that analyzes one image and reapplies its color, tone, and mood across a set. It ships without crops or curves, so it can't yet replace Lightroom, but at $80 per year it targets commercial photographers editing full shoots on set, ahead of a $499-per-year Studio One bundle later in June.

title: Storied Colors – A catalogue of named colors
blurb: Storied Colors is an online catalogue that documents one historical pigment a day, tracing each color's provenance, chemistry, and human cost, from where it was first ground to when it was banned and what replaced it. The site indexes 252 entries so far, adds new ones every Sunday at 06:00 GMT, and amends older entries as conservation labs publish corrections.

title: Vibe Coding for Visualization Implementation: An Empirical Study of Practices and Challenges
blurb: A study of 16 participants using AI "vibe coding" tools to build data visualizations found that natural-language generation lowers the entry barrier but struggles to align user intent with precise visual output. The authors map how practitioners prompt, evaluate, and iterate, and document where the loop breaks down when a chart needs exact encoding rather than an approximate result.

title: The 2026 Core77 Design Awards Toys & Play Winners
blurb: The 2026 Core77 Design Awards named Geni, an AI storytelling toy that lets children build narratives from physical character-and-action blocks, the Professional winner in Toys & Play, with student honors going to ODI, an assistive device for children with psycho-sensory disabilities in physical education. Jury lead Irene Guerrieri said selections prioritized quality of human interaction and inclusivity, weighing play as a fundamental part of life at every age.

title: How a fine-art obsession helped this fantasy indie game attract millions of views
blurb: Sovereign Tower, a solo-built indie RPG from Wild Wits, swaps open-world scale for a single hand-painted tower and an illustration-led art direction that draws on classical fairy-tale etchings and Ivan Bilibin to give every character instant personality. Art director Gobert's advice is the takeaway: nail your project's identity first, keep every asset coherent within one style, and limit your palette to build a strong visual signature.

title: Gaudí's "ghost of an alternative New York" visualised by AI artist Thierry Lechanteur
blurb: Belgian AI artist Thierry Lechanteur rendered Gaudí's unbuilt Hotel Attraction, a supertall New York tower designed around 1908, as a series of digital visuals released for Dezeen's Gaudí Centenary series. The renders reconstruct one of Gaudí's least-known speculative projects from sparse archival sketches, offering a concrete look at a building that was never realized rather than a faithful historical record.

title: Massive breach spills credentials for thousands of sensitive networks
blurb: Researchers disclosed a credential-spraying campaign against internet-facing FortiGate firewalls that compromised nearly 74,000 devices across 194 countries, dumping plaintext credentials for organizations including Oracle, Chevron, Lenovo, FedEx, a NATO defense contractor, and Fortinet itself. Roughly half of all exposed Fortinet firewalls are affected and most remain online with valid credentials, so operators should treat compromised devices as live, rotate credentials, and audit downstream RADIUS and Active Directory access immediately.

title: Microsoft Confirms RoguePlanet Defender Zero-Day, Says Patch is in Development
blurb: Microsoft confirmed a Defender zero-day codenamed RoguePlanet (CVE-2026-50656, CVSS 7.8), a race condition in the Malware Protection Engine that hands attackers a SYSTEM-level shell regardless of whether real-time protection is enabled. No patch has shipped yet, public PoC code from researcher Chaotic Eclipse already exists, and this is the fourth Defender flaw the same researcher has disclosed after BlueHammer, UnDefend, and RedSun.

title: FortiBleed leak exposes Fortinet VPN credentials for 73,000 devices.
blurb: A leak dubbed "FortiBleed," discovered by researcher Bob Diachenko, exposed valid FortiGate SSL VPN credentials, including plaintext passwords, for 73,932 firewall URLs at organizations including Chevron, Samsung, AT&T, and Mercedes-Benz. Diachenko attributes the haul to a Russian-speaking group that intercepted and cracked SSL VPN authentication hashes; affected organizations should treat exposed appliances as compromised, force password resets, and audit Active Directory for lateral movement.

title: CISA Warns of Actively Exploited Joomla JCE Flaw Allowing PHP Code Execution
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-48907 (CVSS 10.0), a maximum-severity improper access control flaw in the Widget Factory Joomla Content Editor (JCE), to its KEV catalog after confirming active exploitation that lets unauthenticated users create editor profiles and upload and execute arbitrary PHP code. The flaw affects JCE versions 1.0.0 through 2.9.99.4 and is patched in 2.9.99.5, with FCEB agencies ordered to remediate by June 19.

title: How hacker group TeamPCP exploited the open source trust model and distribution method to compromise and inject malware into over 1,000 software packages (Matt Kapko/CyberScoop)
blurb: The hacker group TeamPCP compromised over 1,000 open source software packages by abusing the trust and distribution model of public registries to inject malware into legitimate projects. The campaign turned the open source supply chain into a malware delivery vector, so defenders should audit recently updated dependencies, pin known-good versions, and verify package integrity rather than trusting registry availability alone.

title: Splunk Enterprise Vulnerability Exploited in Attacks Days After Disclosure
blurb: Splunk confirmed limited in-the-wild exploitation of CVE-2026-20253, a critical unauthenticated flaw in Splunk Enterprise where the PostgreSQL sidecar service endpoint lacks authentication controls, allowing any network-reachable attacker to create or truncate arbitrary files and achieve RCE. Patches landed June 10 for versions 10.2 before 10.2.4 and 10.0 before 10.0.7; CISA added it to KEV on June 18 with a June 21 federal remediation deadline.

title: AMD silently removes memory encryption from consumer Ryzen CPUs, leaving users unaware that they may be vulnerable — security feature vanishes after newer AGESA firmware, AMD engineers go radio silent when pressed about the change
blurb: AMD appears to have silently removed Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME) from consumer Ryzen CPUs outside its Pro lineup, stripping protection against physical attacks that siphon data from connected memory, with the change traced to AGESA 1.2.7.0 firmware. The removal is undetectable on Windows and hard to spot on Linux, so defenders relying on at-rest memory encryption for consumer endpoints should verify TSME status directly rather than assume it is still active.

title: CISA warns Fortinet users to secure devices after FortiBleed leak
blurb: CISA warned Fortinet customers to secure their appliances after the "FortiBleed" leak exposed plaintext VPN and admin credentials for 73,932 FortiGate firewalls across 21,632 domains and 194 countries, with threat actors already using the data to target internet-facing devices. Affected owners should terminate all SSL VPN and admin sessions, reset VPN and admin passwords, enable phishing-resistant MFA, and review logs for lateral movement.

title: Klue OAuth breach linked to 'Icarus' Salesforce data theft attacks
blurb: Klue's OAuth breach let the "Icarus" extortion group abuse Battlecards integration service accounts and customer-associated OAuth tokens to query the Salesforce REST API and exfiltrate CRM data from multiple organizations, with ReliaQuest and Huntress confirming the incident. Salesforce has disabled the Klue Battlecards integration during the investigation, so affected orgs should revoke Klue OAuth tokens, audit `/services/data/v59.0/query` API activity, and treat exposed CRM data as compromised.

title: F5 issues out-of-band patches for critical NGINX vulnerabilities
blurb: F5 released out-of-band patches for two critical NGINX flaws, CVE-2026-42530 in ngx_http_v3_module and CVE-2026-42055 in the proxy_v2 and gRPC modules, which let unauthenticated remote attackers trigger DoS or RCE via use-after-free or heap overflow on non-default configurations with ASLR disabled or bypassed. Neither is flagged as exploited yet, but admins who can't patch immediately should disable HTTP/3 and remove the ignore_invalid_headers off directive as interim mitigations.

title: I found 10k GitHub repositories distributing Trojan malware
blurb: A researcher uncovered roughly 10,000 GitHub repositories distributing Trojan malware, each cloned from a legitimate project with its commits and contributors copied to appear authentic. The repos add a zip archive link to the readme containing a loader executable and a malicious lua51.dll, re-pushing the same commit every few hours to evade takedown; defenders should treat GitHub readme-hosted archive links as untrusted and block the pattern.

title: Malicious JetBrains Plugins Steal AI API Keys as Chrome Extensions Capture Chatbot Chats
blurb: Fifteen malicious JetBrains Marketplace plugins, posing as DeepSeek-based AI coding assistants, have exfiltrated users' OpenAI, SiliconFlow, and DeepSeek API keys in plaintext to an attacker server (39.107.60.51) since late October 2025. Aikido Security found the plugins work as advertised while siphoning entered keys, with two—CodeGPT AI Assistant and DeepSeek AI Assist—each showing over 25,000 possibly inflated downloads, so audit installs and rotate any keys exposed to them.

title: Apple fixes Beats Studio Buds flaw that let hackers spy on conversations
blurb: Apple patched CVE-2025-20701, a high-severity missing-authentication flaw in the Airoha Bluetooth SoC used in Beats Studio Buds that lets an attacker in Bluetooth range eavesdrop through the microphone of an unpaired device without authentication or pairing. The fix ships in Beats Firmware Update 1B211, auto-delivered when buds are paired and within range of an iPhone, iPad, or Mac; chaining CVE-2025-20700 and CVE-2025-20702 allows full headphone takeover and HFP command injection.

title: Embedding Forbidden Text in Spyware to Discourage AI Analysis
blurb: A malware developer is embedding fake system instructions and policy-triggering text about nuclear and biological weapons into a JavaScript comment block at the top of their spyware, designed to derail LLM-based triage that feeds file headers to a model without isolating them as untrusted data. The trick only affects naive LLM-first pipelines and can cause refusals or premature classification; YARA, entropy checks, AST parsing, and behavioral rules still detect the payload after the comment.

title: F5 Patches Two Critical NGINX Open Source Flaws Enabling Remote Code Execution
blurb: F5 patched two critical RCE flaws in NGINX Open Source, CVE-2026-42530 and CVE-2026-42055 (both CVSS 9.2), each exploitable by remote unauthenticated attackers against the HTTP/3 QUIC module and HTTP/2 proxy modules respectively. Code execution requires ASLR to be disabled or bypassed, but admins who can't upgrade immediately should disable HTTP/3 and remove the ignore_invalid_headers off directive as interim mitigations.

title: Police cleans nearly 15,000 SocGholish-infected sites tied to Evil Corp
blurb: Law enforcement under Operation Endgame cleaned SocGholish malware from 14,971 compromised WordPress sites and took 106 servers and domains offline, disrupting an infection chain tied to the Evil Corp cybercrime group. SocGholish (also tracked as FakeUpdates) hijacks legitimate WordPress sites to push fake browser updates that deliver Dridex, ransomware, and other payloads, so affected owners should rotate credentials, enable MFA, and remove unknown admin accounts.

title: ShapedPlugin update flow hacked to infect WordPress sites
blurb: Attackers compromised ShapedPlugin's official update channel to push backdoored builds of three paid WordPress plugins (Product Slider Pro, Real Testimonials Pro, and Smart Post Show Pro) to paying customers between May 21 and the June 16 disclosure. The malware installs a hidden plugin impersonating WooCommerce that steals login credentials, 2FA secrets, wp-config.php keys, and recent order data, so affected sites should rotate all secrets and audit for rogue plugins.

title: Cybersecurity Firms Impacted by Klue Supply Chain Attack
blurb: A supply chain attack on market intelligence platform Klue, beginning June 11, let attackers push a malicious code update that harvested customer OAuth tokens and abused the Salesforce REST API to exfiltrate CRM data. Huntress and Recorded Future confirmed impact limited to business contact and sales data; Klue revoked all OAuth tokens and Salesforce disabled the Klue Battlecards app on June 17.

title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
blurb: Researchers from multiple security firms linked the Popa botnet, which has hijacked millions of Android TV boxes into a residential-proxy network for the past four years, to NetNut, a proxy provider run by publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies (NASDAQ: ALAR). Popa operates as a plugin of the Vo1d malware campaign, scattering ad fraud, account takeovers, and data-scraping traffic across more than 1.4 million IP addresses.

title: FortiBleed campaign exposes 75,000 Fortinet firewalls worldwide
blurb: The FortiBleed campaign exposed working administrator and SSL VPN credentials for roughly 75,000 internet-facing FortiGate firewalls across 194 countries, about half of all Fortinet firewalls visible on Shodan. Researchers including Kevin Beaumont and Hudson Rock attribute the harvest to credentials recovered from stolen configuration files over time, so patching alone is insufficient; defenders should rotate all FortiGate admin and VPN credentials and audit configs for prior compromise.

title: MosaicLeaks: Can your research agent keep a secret?
blurb: MosaicLeaks introduces a deep-research benchmark showing that agents combining private documents with web retrieval leak sensitive enterprise data through their outbound query logs, where an observer reassembles fragments via the mosaic effect without ever seeing the documents. The authors found leakage is common across tested models and that training purely for task accuracy worsens it. Their privacy-aware RL method, PA-DR, cut answer and full-information leakage from 34.0% to 9.9% while raising strict chain success from 48.7% to 58.7%.

title: Salesforce Disables Klue App Integration After OAuth Token Abuse Exposes Customer Data
blurb: Salesforce disabled the Klue Battlecards app integration after the Icarus extortion group compromised a legacy integration credential at Klue, harvested OAuth tokens via a malicious code update, and accessed customer Salesforce data including business contacts and price quotes from victims like Huntress. The breach stemmed from Klue's connection rather than a Salesforce platform flaw, so defenders running Klue or similar third-party integrations should revoke and rotate affected OAuth tokens and audit connected-app access for anomalous activity.

title: DragonForce Hackers Abuse Microsoft Teams Relays to Hide Backdoor.Turn C2 Traffic
blurb: DragonForce ransomware operators deployed Backdoor.Turn, a custom Go RAT that hides C2 traffic by acquiring an anonymous Teams visitor token, routing through a legitimate Microsoft TURN relay, then running a QUIC session to the attacker's server. Defenders saw only outbound connections to Microsoft Teams during the one-to-two-month dwell; watch for DLL side-loading, BYOVD driver abuse (including a Huawei audio driver), and injection into DbgView64.exe.

title: Gentlemen ransomware uses multiple EDR killers to disable defenses
blurb: Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service maintains a suite of EDR killers, led by the custom GentleKiller tool, which uses bring-your-own-vulnerable-driver (BYOVD) to gain kernel privileges and disable security engines, per ESET. GentleKiller spans at least eight variants targeting 400-plus processes across roughly 48 vendors including CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, and Microsoft, and its modular design lets affiliates swap drivers to weaponize newly disclosed flaws without major code changes.

title: Microsoft discovers new lightweight backdoor that steals cryptocurrency
blurb: Microsoft disclosed Crypto Clipper, a self-propagating worm that spreads via .lnk files on USB drives and monitors clipboards for wallet addresses and seed phrases, exfiltrating stolen credentials and timed screenshots over Tor through a local SOCKS5 proxy. By bundling a portable Tor client and IP-less C2 with remote code execution, the malware doubles as a lightweight backdoor, so defenders should restrict USB autorun, block unauthorized Tor traffic, and monitor for clipboard-hijacking behavior.

title: Build your own vulnerability harness
blurb: Cloudflare details how it built a model-agnostic vulnerability harness for Project Glasswing, deliberately swapping models across the pipeline so one model handles discovery and a different one validates, cross-checking findings through distinct reasoning. The architecture persists hundreds of investigations across runs, traces vulnerabilities through cross-repo dependencies, and filters thousands of raw candidates down to a triaged queue of actionable fixes.

title: Microsoft found malware that hijacks crypto wallets and spreads through USB sticks
blurb: Microsoft disclosed a clipboard-hijacking worm it tracks as Trojan:Win32/CryptoBandits, spreading via malicious .lnk shortcuts on infected USB drives since February to steal crypto wallet seed phrases and private keys. It polls the Windows clipboard roughly every 500ms, exfiltrates captured keys and screenshots over Tor, and silently swaps copied recipient addresses for attacker-controlled ones; defenders should disable AutoRun, block .lnk execution on removable media, and restrict script hosts.

title: 15,000 WordPress Websites Cleaned Up in SocGholish Botnet Takedown
blurb: Law enforcement in the Netherlands, Canada, the US, and Germany, working with Europol, dismantled the SocGholish (FakeUpdates) botnet, taking down 106 C&C servers and cleaning malware from 14,971 compromised WordPress sites. Operated by initial-access broker TA569 (UNC1543), the JavaScript dropper has seeded LockBit, RansomHub, and AsyncRAT via fake browser updates since 2017, so site owners should rotate credentials, enable MFA, and audit for leftover backdoors.

title: NAMESAKES: Probing Identity Memorization in Text-to-Image Models
blurb: This paper introduces a fully black-box probe that detects whether a text-to-image model has memorized a real person's likeness, requiring no reference photos, training-data access, or model internals. Tested on the new NAMESAKES dataset of over 1,000 public-figure names across fame levels, the probe reliably separates memorized identities from fabricated ones. For teams assessing T2I deployments, it offers a practical way to audit privacy exposure without white-box access to the model.

title: Junior Hacker Used Tailscale and OpenSSH to Keep Access After His C2 Went Offline
blurb: Cato Networks tracked a junior French-speaking operator nicknamed "Poisson" through a 33-day intrusion at a small French automotive firm, capturing 339 commands after he left SSH keys and a playbook in an open Backblaze B2 bucket. Before his Havoc C2 went dark, he installed OpenSSH and Tailscale on a victim machine for out-of-band persistence, so taking the C2 offline failed to remediate the compromise.

title: Sweeping Credential-Harvesting Heist Compromises +30K Fortinet Devices
blurb: A Russian-speaking threat group has compromised more than 30,791 internet-facing Fortinet firewalls and VPN gateways across 194 countries, with SOCRadar finding a verified repository of working credentials after discovering the attackers' exposed operational server. Researchers attribute this to credential compromise rather than a Fortinet flaw, with telecom, government, and critical infrastructure hardest hit, so defenders should rotate credentials on default, built-in, and long-lived admin accounts and enforce MFA on edge appliances.

title: FloatDoor: Platform-Triggered Backdoors in LLMs
blurb: FloatDoor is the first input-independent, platform-triggered backdoor for LLMs, using two lightweight LoRA adapters to make a model behave maliciously only when served on a target hardware platform while remaining benign elsewhere and preserving aggregate utility. Demonstrated on Qwen3-4B across NVIDIA GPUs, Google TPUs, AWS Graviton, and Alibaba Yitian-710, it exploits the time-of-check/time-of-use gap between model auditing and serving to reliably induce exploitable code vulnerabilities on a chosen platform.

title: Massive password-stealing attack hits 75k Fortinet firewalls
blurb: A credential-harvesting campaign has compromised roughly 75,000 internet-facing Fortinet firewalls, scraping administrator and VPN passwords from exposed appliances. Operators running affected FortiGate devices on the edge should assume captured credentials are already in adversary hands, rotate all admin and VPN secrets, and audit logs for unauthorized configuration changes and rogue accounts.

title: AWS Security Agent adds threat modeling, Kiro power and Claude Code plugin, and more
blurb: AWS Security Agent, part of AWS Continuum, added STRIDE-based threat modeling and full-repository code review (both in preview), with pull request scanning, remediation commits, and integrations for GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and Confluence. A new MCP integration plus a Claude Code plugin and Kiro support let teams run code reviews, generate threat models, and apply fixes inline from any IDE or CLI.

title: Authorities disrupt Evil Corp’s SocGholish botnet
blurb: A multinational law enforcement operation disrupted SocGholish (aka FakeUpdates), the Evil Corp-linked malware framework that has compromised mostly WordPress sites since 2017 to redirect users through traffic distribution systems and broker initial access for DoppelPaymer, LockBit, RansomHub, and other ransomware. Authorities from the US, Canada, Germany, the Netherlands, and Europol seized 106 servers and remediated nearly 15,000 infected sites, and the FBI issued a PSA warning defenders about TDS-based intrusion chains.

title: Apple Patches Beats Studio Buds Flaw Letting Nearby Attackers Spy via Microphone
blurb: Apple released Beats Firmware Update 1B211 to patch CVE-2025-20701 (CVSS 8.8), an incorrect-authorization flaw in the Airoha Bluetooth audio SDK that lets an attacker within Bluetooth range pair with an unpaired Beats Studio Buds and listen through its microphone without user interaction. ERNW researchers disclosed the bug alongside two other Airoha SoC flaws at TROOPERS in June 2025, and Jabra shipped similar fixes in December.

title: Microsoft Details Windows Clipper Malware Campaign Using USB LNK Worm and Tor-Based C2
blurb: Microsoft disclosed a Windows cryptocurrency clipper campaign active since February 2026 that spreads via malicious USB LNK files, intercepts clipboard data to swap crypto wallet addresses, and routes C2 traffic through a bundled Tor client over a local SOCKS5 proxy. The worm hides legitimate documents behind decoy shortcuts, persists via scheduled tasks, and exits if Task Manager is running, blending wallet theft with screenshot exfiltration and remote code execution.

title: Accenture to Acquire Majority Stake in Dragos, All of runZero, NetRise in $4.1 Billion OT Cybersecurity Push
blurb: Accenture announced it will acquire a majority stake in OT security firm Dragos and fully buy runZero and NetRise in a deal valued at roughly $4.1B, with runZero and NetRise to operate under an independent Dragos. The move expands Accenture's industrial cybersecurity reach and reads as a competitive response to ServiceNow's $7.75B acquisition of Armis.

title: Majority of Internet-Accessible REDCap Servers Outdated
blurb: Censys found that roughly 8,500 internet-exposed REDCap clinical-research servers are running outdated software, with just over 1% on the latest 17.1.3 release and 30% still on version 16.0.17. Google's GTIG reports the China-linked actor UNC6508 has targeted legacy REDCap instances since September 2023, deploying credential-harvesting malware and the InfiniteRed backdoor against US medical, academic, and military research orgs; operators should inventory deployments and patch to the current version.

title: AI Use by the US Government
blurb: The OMB disclosed 3,611 active or planned federal AI use cases as of April 14, a 70% increase over the final Biden-era inventory, spanning grant-application ideology screening at HHS, inmate-misconduct prediction at the Bureau of Prisons, and reactor control at the Department of Energy. The author argues most of these programs could be implemented responsibly, and that the real objections trace to the underlying policies rather than the use of automation itself.

title: Crypto Clipper Campaign Abuses Fake Reviews, AI Narrators, and VirusTotal Comments
blurb: A Check Point–tracked threat actor is distributing a Rust-based cryptocurrency clipper that hijacks the clipboard on Windows and macOS, swapping copied wallet addresses for attacker-controlled ones, hidden inside Solana and Pump.fun sniper bots and crash-game predictors. The campaign relies on Ghost Networks to manufacture trust, using coordinated VirusTotal upvotes and comments, six-plus GitHub accounts, and inflated SourceForge and download counts to make the malware appear reputable before victims click.

title: USB worm spreads crypto-stealing malware via Windows shortcut files
blurb: Microsoft detailed a USB worm campaign, active since at least February, that spreads clipper malware via LNK shortcut files on removable drives, replacing copied crypto wallet addresses in the clipboard with attacker-controlled ones chosen to visually mimic the originals. The malware also harvests BIP39 seed phrases and private keys, captures screenshots, supports C2-triggered RCE, and exfiltrates over Tor, so defenders should hunt behaviorally and disable USB autorun and scheduled-task persistence rather than rely on signatures.

title: Law enforcement hits SocGholish: 106 servers down, 15,000 sites cleaned
blurb: Operation Endgame, the international law enforcement coalition, seized 106 servers and domains tied to SocGholish and cleaned nearly 15,000 compromised websites used to serve its fake-browser-update lures. The operation, run by Evil Corp-linked TA569, has pushed infostealers and RATs via injected WordPress JavaScript since 2017, and Infoblox warns the disruption's longevity is unproven, so defenders should keep hunting for SocGholish callbacks.

title: Copilot searched your mailbox. LiteLLM handed out admin keys. Run this 5-check audit before your stack is next
blurb: Varonis disclosed SearchLeak (CVE-2026-42824), a zero-click exfiltration chain in Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search that lets a crafted microsoft.com URL inject prompts, bypass output sanitization via a rendering race, and route mailbox data out through a Bing SSRF. Four days earlier, Obsidian Security chained three LiteLLM flaws (CVSS 9.9) from a default low-privilege account to proxy admin and RCE; both vendors have patched.

title: FIFA Bug Exposes World Cup Streams to Remote Takeover
blurb: An ethical hacker known as BobDaHacker registered through FIFA's public Agent Platform and reached the same Microsoft Entra tenant backing FIFA's internal systems, gaining control over World Cup television streams and match management systems. The flaw was client-side authorization with no server-side enforcement: the frontend returned access-denied while the backend API served any authenticated user, so defenders should validate authorization on every API call, not just in the UI.

title: When Lower Privileges Suffice: Investigating Over-Privileged Tool Selection in LLM Agents
blurb: This paper introduces ToolPrivBench, a benchmark measuring whether LLM agents pick higher-privilege tools when a sufficient lower-privilege alternative exists. Across eight domains and five risk patterns, the authors find over-privileged selection is common in mainstream agents and worsens after transient tool failures, and that general safety alignment and prompt-level guardrails don't reliably enforce least privilege. Their privilege-aware post-training defense substantially cut unnecessary high-privilege tool use while preserving capability, pointing to alignment rather than prompts as the durable control for agentic tool access.

title: Cisco to Acquire WideField Security to Boost Splunk’s Agentic SOC
blurb: Cisco announced an agreement to acquire identity lifecycle security firm WideField Security to feed deeper identity and session intelligence into Splunk's Agentic SOC and Cisco's broader data fabric. Terms were not disclosed; this marks Cisco's third cybersecurity acquisition of 2026 after Galileo and Astrix, landing the same day Accenture revealed $4.1B in OT security deals for Dragos, runZero, and NetRise.

title: Attackers hit pair of critical Fortinet vulnerabilities the vendor disclosed in April
blurb: Attackers are actively exploiting two critical FortiSandbox flaws Fortinet disclosed and patched in April: CVE-2026-39808 (OS-command injection) and CVE-2026-39813 (path traversal), with a third, CVE-2026-25089, also under attack. VulnCheck and Defused observed 49 exploitation events from 11 IPs over six days, and the current verification and reconnaissance activity typically precedes a heavier wave, so patch affected appliances now.

title: Texas government data breach allowed hackers to steal 3 million driver’s licenses and passports
blurb: Texas Parks & Wildlife disclosed a breach of its hunting and fishing license vendor that exposed the driver's license and passport numbers of more than 3 million people, alongside email addresses, phone numbers, and home addresses. The department has not named the compromised vendor or specified when the intrusion occurred, leaving affected residents exposed to identity theft and document fraud with no timeline for remediation.

title: Apple patches high-severity eavesdropping vulnerability in Beats Studio Buds
blurb: Apple patched CVE-2025-20701 (CVSS 8.8), an improper-authentication flaw in the Airoha Bluetooth chip firmware of Beats Studio Buds that lets an attacker within radio range impersonate a previously paired device and eavesdrop through the phone's microphone. The fix ships in Beats Firmware Update 1B211, delivered automatically when the buds are paired with a nearby iPhone, iPad, or Mac; Jabra, Bose, and JBL have shipped the same Airoha SDK patch.

title: Atlassian, Splunk Patch Critical Vulnerabilities
blurb: Splunk patched CVE-2026-20266 (CVSS 9.1), a command-injection flaw in its AI Toolkit that lets authenticated admins execute arbitrary OS commands via an unsafe shell pattern in the btool config helper, fixed in version 5.7.4. Atlassian shipped 100 bulletins resolving critical third-party dependency flaws in Axios, Apache Tomcat, and Netty across Jira, Confluence, Bitbucket, and Bamboo; admins unable to upgrade the AI Toolkit should uninstall it.

title: Accenture shells out $4.18B on three companies in big industrial cybersecurity push
blurb: Accenture announced it will acquire a majority stake in OT security firm Dragos for $3.25B and buy asset-discovery vendor runZero and firmware-visibility vendor NetRise outright, a combined $4.18B bet on defending power grids, pipelines, and factories. The deal pushes Accenture from OT services into OT security software at scale, with Dragos CEO Robert M. Lee leading the combined business as an independent unit.

title: US holds off blacklisting DeepSeek, more than 100 firms deemed security risks
blurb: The Trump administration declined to add DeepSeek to the Commerce Department's Entity List, holding off on export restrictions that would bar the Chinese AI firm from buying US technology. The decision came alongside a broader review that flagged more than 100 companies as national security risks, signaling internal disagreement over how aggressively to target China's AI sector.

title: npm v12’s Biggest Security Change: From Implicit to Explicit Trust
blurb: npm v12, expected July 2026, shifts package installation from implicit to explicit trust by blocking three high-risk vectors by default: install scripts (allowScripts), Git-repository installs (--allow-git), and remote-URL installs (--allow-remote). JFrog's research team ties these mechanisms to roughly 53% of malicious npm attacks over the past year, including multiple Shai-Hulud variants that abused install-time execution to steal credentials and pivot into CI/CD. Teams should audit builds for dependencies relying on these behaviors before upgrading.

title: Windows update leaves third-party Office document launches in limbo
blurb: A recent Windows update broke the file-association handlers that let third-party applications launch Microsoft Office documents, leaving those apps unable to open files they previously handled. Microsoft has acknowledged the regression but not yet shipped a fix, so admins relying on non-Microsoft document workflows should hold the update or prepare a rollback until a patch lands.

title: From Efficiency to Leakage -- Privacy Backdoor in Federated Language Model Fine-Tuning
blurb: A malicious parameter server can corrupt a PEFT adapter into a privacy backdoor that memorizes each client training sample in an isolated neuron, then analytically inverts those per-sample updates to reconstruct the original text without degrading model utility. The attack, NeuroImprint, recovered 59% to 79% of fine-tuning samples with high semantic fidelity across BERT, GPT-2, Qwen2, and Llama3.2, exposing federated PEFT setups that trust a central server.

title: Many Let's Encrypt renewals had errors today
blurb: Let's Encrypt reported renewal errors on its ACME v02 production API today, causing certificate issuance and renewal failures for affected clients before services returned to operational status. Operators who hit failed renewals should re-run their ACME clients now that the incident has cleared and verify expiry dates on any certificates that missed their renewal window.

title: 144 Mastra npm Packages Compromised via Hijacked Contributor Account
blurb: Attackers hijacked the dormant npm account of a former Mastra contributor ("ehindero") and mass-published 144 malicious "@mastra/*" packages on June 17, each adding a tainted "easy-day-js" dependency whose postinstall hook drops a cross-platform infostealer targeting browser history and 160+ crypto wallet extensions. Audit Mastra installs from the past 24 hours, rotate any exposed credentials and wallet keys, and pin to releases carrying SLSA provenance attestations, since the attacker published from a personal token without them.

title: 칼럼 | 미토스가 바꾼 것은 공격 방식이 아니다, 대응 시간이다
blurb: The author argues that Anthropic's Mythos model has collapsed the defender's response window, not by inventing new attacks but by running familiar ones at machine speed: it found 2,000+ previously unknown vulnerabilities across major operating systems in seven weeks and wrote working exploits without human direction. Over 99% of those flaws remain unpatched, and the author warns that identity infrastructure is itself code, making KYC, biometric, and mobile-license logic part of the attack surface.

title: AWS unveils agent security, data access tools
blurb: AWS released a set of agent security and data-access tools at its re:Inforce conference, aimed at controlling what AI agents can read and act on across enterprise environments. The launch leans on Anthropic's Mythos model to surface vulnerabilities faster, positioning the tooling for teams already running agentic workflows on AWS infrastructure.

title: Microsoft fixes Windows Server 2016 security update failures
blurb: Microsoft resolved a known issue that caused June 2026 security updates to fail with 0x80070002 (FILE_NOT_FOUND) errors on Windows Server 2016 systems, which primarily hit devices installing KB5094122 without first applying last month's KB5087537. Affected devices should no longer see installation failures when deploying the June update, though admins should confirm the prerequisite KB is present before redeploying KB5094122.

title: Salesforce Data Thefts Continue via Klue App Compromise
blurb: Threat actors abused OAuth tokens from Klue's Battlecards app to access connected Salesforce instances and exfiltrate customer data, with victims including cybersecurity vendor Huntress. Salesforce suspended the integration on June 17 after detecting unauthorized access; the attack follows the same third-party OAuth-abuse playbook as the Salesloft Drift and Gainsight compromises, so defenders should audit and revoke OAuth tokens for all connected SaaS integrations.

title: Rokarolla Banking Trojan Targets 200 Applications
blurb: Zimperium disclosed Rokarolla, a new Android banking trojan that uses screen overlays to steal credentials from 217 banking and cryptocurrency apps and abuses Accessibility Services to capture lockscreen PINs, keystrokes, SMS, and screenshots. Distributed via fake Chrome and TikTok installers posing as Google Play Protect, it disables Play Protect, hides its icon, and mutes audio to suppress security alerts during fraud.

title: Before SpaceX IPO, investors in China secretly acquired stakes
blurb: A private investor list obtained by ProPublica shows that a businessman tied to Chinese military contractors and an entity linked to the Qatari royal family acquired stakes in SpaceX years before its IPO, routed through a US middleman firm called Tomales Bay Capital. The disclosures matter because SpaceX builds spy satellites for the Pentagon, and the company barred Chinese and Hong Kong investors from its IPO last week over regulatory and compliance risks.

title: Analyzing Defensive Misdirection Against Model-Guided Automated Attacks on Agentic AI Systems
blurb: This paper argues that conventional detect-and-block defenses against prompt injection and jailbreaks can let attacker success rate approach 100% as query budgets grow, because predictable refusals give model-guided attack tools clean feedback to optimize against. The authors propose detect-and-misdirect: returning safe but deliberately misleading responses to poison the attacker's automated judge. Their proof-of-concept, CMPE, cut estimated ASR upper bounds by up to two orders of magnitude and nearly eliminated verified successes in PAIR and GPTFuzz runs.

title: Apple collects every tap to deliver App Store personalized recommendations
blurb: Security researchers at Mysk found that Apple's App Store logs every tap users make to power its new Personalized Collections feature, capturing identifiable analytics that can even derive a user's typing speed during searches, with no setting to disable the collection. The feature began rolling out this week in the U.S., and Apple currently offers no opt-out toggle for the underlying data capture.

title: How software development’s speed obsession enabled TeamPCP’s chaos crusade
blurb: TeamPCP has compromised and injected malicious code into more than 1,000 open-source packages in under four months, starting with Trivy in February, exploiting the industry's unverified trust in third-party dependencies. The scale rides on automated CI/CD pipelines and AI coding agents that install unvetted packages with no human in the loop. The tactics aren't novel; defenders should enforce dependency pinning, signature verification, and gating of agent-installed packages before ingestion.

title: DISARM: Target Electronic Device Informed Mitigation of Software Runtime Side-Channel Vulnerabilities
blurb: DISARM is a joint hardware-software methodology for mitigating runtime timing side-channels that uses measured timing values from real embedded devices to generate targeted software fixes, avoiding the over-fixing and under-fixing that plague hardware-agnostic tools. The authors validate it across 22 benchmarks for C, C++, and Java, reporting lower execution-time and code-size overhead with better correctness than PENDULUM and DifFuzzAR on five embedded and edge devices.

title: Volkswagen started blocking GrapheneOS users
blurb: Volkswagen's myVW app began blocking GrapheneOS users, joining a growing list of apps that reject the hardened Android fork over Play Integrity attestation checks. Affected owners report partial workarounds involving fresh Play Store installs and temporarily enabling Google Play services, but the trend signals tightening app-side device attestation that increasingly locks out privacy-focused and de-Googled mobile setups.

title: Kaspersky finds malware hidden in Steam Wallpaper Engine that hijacks accounts to spread itself — dozens of malicious packages downloaded tens of thousands of times
blurb: Kaspersky disclosed a malware campaign abusing Wallpaper Engine's "application wallpaper" executable feature on Steam, deploying infostealers, crypto miners, and ransomware that hijack victims' Steam sessions to upload more infected packages under their names. Running since late 2025 and concentrated on gamers in China (89% of attempts), the campaign self-propagates through stolen accounts, with dozens of malicious packages downloaded tens of thousands of times before takedown.

title: Analyzing the Narration Gap in LLM-Solver Loops
blurb: Solver-backed LLM pipelines can produce a sound verdict yet still let prompt injection invert the answer the user actually reads.

title: INC Ransomware Thrives by Mastering the Basics
blurb: INC, a ransomware-as-a-service group that emerged in 2023, has claimed more than 800 victims by mastering proven intrusion methods rather than novel ones, exploiting Citrix Bleed 2 (CVE-2025-5777), SimpleHelp (CVE-2024-57727), Citrix NetScaler (CVE-2023-3519), and Fortinet EMS (CVE-2023-48788) per Acronis research. Defenders should prioritize patching these known flaws and lock down valid-account access, since INC aggressively targets healthcare and other high-pressure sectors to maximize extortion leverage.

title: When Global Gating Is Enough: Admission-Time Hubness Control in Anisotropic Vector Retrieval Systems
blurb: This paper proposes admission-time hubness control to defend RAG systems against poisoning, where a single injected document becomes the nearest neighbor for many unrelated queries. Rather than periodic reverse-kNN scans that leave an exposure window, the authors score each candidate against sentinel queries and quarantine hub-like documents before insertion. A single global gate hit recall 1.0 at the decisive embedding point and 0.91 on HotFlip attacks with 1% false positives, adding roughly 3.1% to HNSW ingestion latency and scaling flat to 10^6 vectors.

title: Mastodon 4.6 adds profile Collections and two-factor controls
blurb: Mastodon 4.6 ships Collections, letting users assemble curated lists of up to 25 profiles and share them by link, with opt-in controls, per-add notifications, and self-removal to limit spam and abuse. The release also gives server administrators a control to require two-factor authentication on member accounts, which infosec.exchange and its sibling servers plan to enforce.

title: Calibration Without Comprehension: Diagnosing the Limits of Fine-Tuning LLMs for Vulnerability Detection in Systems Software
blurb: A new study, CWE-Trace, evaluated eight base LLMs and 15 LoRA fine-tuned variants on 834 curated Linux kernel samples across 74 CWEs, using a strict temporal split to rule out data leakage. Fine-tuning only shifted output thresholds without changing decision policy ("calibration without comprehension"), and contamination gave no measurable edge. Best detection hit just 52.1%, barely above chance, with exact CWE ranking below 1.3% Top-1. Treat LLM vuln-detection output as a weak signal, not a reasoning engine.

title: Google sets timeline for Android developer verification enforcement
blurb: Google will begin enforcing Android developer verification on September 30, 2026, starting with users in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand before expanding to certified Android devices globally in 2027. Apps distributed through participating stores including Google Play, Galaxy Store, and Xiaomi's GetApps must be linked to a verified developer identity, raising the bar for actors distributing malware anonymously.

title: Novo Nordisk Breach Exposes Software Development Pipeline Risk
blurb: A leaked GitHub token tied to Novo Nordisk's software development pipeline gave attackers access to the company's source code repositories, exposing how a single exposed credential can compromise the build chain. The breach reflects a common failure to treat secrets as identities subject to rotation, scoping, and revocation, rather than as static strings managed by tooling.

title: UK Social Media Ban for Minors Has Privacy Experts Worried
blurb: The UK will ban under-16s from user-to-user social platforms like Instagram, TikTok, and X starting in early 2027, with possible limits on infinite scrolling and digital curfews for under-18s. Privacy experts warn the mandate could push platforms toward intrusive age-verification that normalizes identity collection and surveillance, mirroring bans already in force in Australia and proposed in Canada and several EU states.

title: Passwords nicked for nearly 74,000 Fortinet devices
blurb: Hudson Rock disclosed "Fortibleed," a campaign in which an automated brute-force and SSL VPN hash-cracking operation leaked working credentials for 73,932 Fortinet firewall and VPN devices, with operators pivoting into internal Active Directory to establish persistence. Fortinet disputes the framing, attributing the data to reshared prior breaches and brute-forcing rather than a new incident, but organizations on the affected list should rotate credentials and audit VPN and AD access now.

title: Frontier Airlines site leaks all personal info with just a glance at a boarding pass, researcher claims — booking number and last name nets you every passenger's personal info, including address, passport, TSA PreCheck, and most credit card info
blurb: A researcher disclosed vulnerabilities in Frontier Airlines' API and website that let anyone with a passenger's PNR booking code and last name, both printed on a boarding pass, retrieve full passenger records, including home address, date of birth, passport details, partial credit card data, payment history, and TSA PreCheck Known Traveler Number. The six-digit PNR is brute-forceable, and Frontier's only fix after disclosure was requiring the last name, leaving the exposure largely unaddressed.

title: Quantization as a Malicious Task: Removing Quantization-Conditioned Backdoors via Task Arithmetic
blurb: This paper introduces QVec, a defense against quantization-conditioned backdoors (QCBs), where a model behaves benignly in full precision but activates malicious behavior once quantized for edge deployment. The authors treat the weight difference between the full-precision and quantized models as a structured malicious task vector and subtract it via task arithmetic, requiring no retraining, no trigger samples, and a single quantization pass. Across image classifiers and multiple LLM attack scenarios, QVec suppressed backdoor activation while preserving clean accuracy.

title: How Hackers Found a Back Door Into the American Living Room
blurb: Nation-state attackers are increasingly routing operations through residential proxy networks built from compromised consumer electronics like smart TVs and routers, blending malicious traffic into ordinary household IP space to evade geofencing and reputation-based detection. Defenders should treat residential IP ranges as a viable origin for targeted intrusions and prioritize behavioral and identity-based signals over IP reputation, since proxied traffic from American homes will pass most geolocation filters.

title: Dream Raises $260 Million at $3 Billion Valuation
blurb: Dream raised $260M at a $3B valuation, co-led by Bicycle Capital and Group 11, bringing total funding past $410M for the Israeli sovereign-AI cyber defense startup founded in 2023 by former NSO Group CEO Shalev Hulio and former Austrian Chancellor Sebastian Kurz. The funds will expand deployment of its three national-defense platforms (Sphere, Hero, and Atlas) across Europe, the Middle East, Asia, and the Americas.

title: INC Ransomware Emerges as Major RaaS Threat in 2026 with 830+ Victims Since 2023
blurb: INC ransomware has claimed 830+ victims since August 2023, expanding into a top-tier RaaS operation as affiliates migrated from the disrupted LockBit and BlackCat, with US organizations making up over 65% of listed targets. Affiliates gain initial access via unpatched edge devices like Citrix NetScaler (CVE-2025-5777) and SimpleHelp (CVE-2024-57727), dump Veeam backup credentials, and use BYOVD plus LOLBins before deploying Rust-rewritten encryptors.

title: Sovereign Execution Brokers: Enforcing Certificate-Bound Authority in Agentic Control Planes
blurb: This paper introduces the Sovereign Execution Broker (SEB), a runtime enforcement boundary that sits between an agent's proposed action and the production API call, ensuring mutations only execute against a valid, certificate-bound contract. SEB verifies the requested mutation matches the certified execution contract, checks validity windows and revocation epochs, detects live-state drift, then mints short-lived scoped identity and logs signed decision records. The model only holds if production mutation APIs reject any non-broker identity, which is the deployment burden it places on operators.

title: ChatGPT's image generator can be manipulated to produce violent, sexual content
blurb: Mindgard research found that ChatGPT's image generator can be coaxed into producing graphic violent and sexually explicit content, including depictions of murdered women, in response to generic "restore this photo" style prompts that never request such material. The findings show OpenAI's content filters can be bypassed without a direct malicious request, and that previously reported nudity and face-swap abuses remain only partially mitigated rather than resolved.

title: A Measurement Study of Cryptographic Misuse in Embodied AI Mobile Applications
blurb: Researchers published the first large-scale measurement study of cryptographic misuse in embodied AI mobile apps, analyzing 507 applications (EAIAppZoo) and surfacing 12,975 misuse findings at 80.74% precision. The flaws are structural rather than random: latency-sensitive control paths weaken transport encryption, while offline provisioning and legacy IoT SDKs drive hardcoded credentials. The authors show these mobile-side weaknesses bypass network protections, letting adversaries intercept command channels and hijack physical control of EAI devices.

title: PUFFERDOS: Efficient and Effective Attack String Generation for Regular Expression Denial of Service Vulnerabilities
blurb: This paper introduces PUFFERDOS, a ReDoS attack-string generator that synthesizes inputs feasible within realistic length budgets and validates them at the program level, addressing prior tools that produced strings too long or unreliable to trigger vulnerable regexes in real programs. It defines three vulnerable regex patterns, then refines candidates with ReDoS-specific compositional concolic execution to guarantee real-world exploitability. The takeaway for defenders: pair regex linting with program-level validation, since static pattern-matching alone overstates which ReDoS findings are actually exploitable.

title: Companies are discarding the logs they need to catch a breach
blurb: Half of enterprises drop or never collect an average of 86% of their logs to cut costs, according to a Dynatrace survey of 450 IT leaders, and many retain the rest only briefly. Since intrusions often sit undetected for weeks, investigators reaching for the trail find the relevant entries sampled away or aged out. The retention decision typically sits with observability or platform teams chasing spending targets, outside security's control.

title: I told them forced consent was unlawful. 5 years later it cost Elkjop €1.8M
blurb: Norway's data protection authority fined Elkjop €1.8M for forced consent, after the electronics retailer required Nordic customer-club members to cancel their membership to stop receiving marketing emails. The case began with a 2021 complaint to Sweden's IMY (reference DI-2021-6660), establishing that bundling GDPR Article 21(2) opt-out rights into club membership violates the freely-given consent standard under Articles 4(11) and 7.

title: G-Lox: Group-Adaptive, Privacy-Preserving Bridge Distribution with Two-Party Computation
blurb: G-Lox extends Lox bridge distribution with two-server MPC, hiding group-to-bridge assignments behind a privacy wall at low-KiB per-iteration overhead.

title: Multi-View Decompilation for LLM-Based Malware Classification
blurb: This paper argues that LLM-based malware triage on decompiled pseudo-C is fragile when it relies on a single decompiler, since tools like Ghidra and RetDec are lossy and expose different artifacts of the same binary. The authors built a benchmark of benign utilities and malicious programs decompiled with both tools, and found that feeding LLMs both views raises malicious-class F1, mostly by improving recall. Multi-decompiler prompting is a training-free way to cut false negatives in LLM-assisted triage.

title: Nintendo confirms data stolen in WebMD subsidiary cyberattack
blurb: Nintendo of America confirmed that attackers stole internal employee survey data from TinyPulse, a third-party feedback platform owned by WebMD Health Services, while stating its own systems were not breached and no customer or financial data was accessed. The Shadowbyt3$ extortion group claims it exfiltrated roughly 1GB including names, emails, bank statements, and W-9 forms spanning 2016–2026, and is demanding a $2M ransom.

title: Secure Coding Drift in LLM-Assisted Post-Quantum Cryptography Development: A Gamified Fix
blurb: This paper introduces "Secure Coding Drift," a model for how sustained reliance on LLM-generated code gradually erodes secure coding practices in post-quantum cryptography work, where constant-time execution, side-channel resistance, and exact parametrisation are mandatory. The authors frame the risk as a longitudinal behavioral phenomenon rather than a static vulnerability, and propose a gamified framework that embeds adversarial evaluation, behavioral feedback, and security scoring into the workflow to turn the LLM into an active reviewer rather than a passive code generator.

title: Jamf announces new native AI Governance control plane for macOS
blurb: Jamf announced AI Governance, an upcoming Jamf Pro capability that gives IT and security teams OS-level visibility and control over generative AI tools running on managed Macs, including local models and developer tooling that network proxies miss. It offers deep tool discovery, granular policy controls across model access, network, and file system, and Model Context Protocol server restrictions, plus a vendor engine that tracks new controls automatically.

title: Google told researcher 'Nice catch!' Then denied bug bounty for flaw it still hasn't fixed
blurb: Security researcher Justin O'Leary disclosed ConfigConfusion, a flaw in Google's open-source Config Connector Kubernetes operator that lets any service account with org-level permissions bypass GCP IAM checks and gain roles/owner over an entire organization. Google accepted the report at P1/S1 severity and told O'Leary "Nice catch," then reversed course to deny that a vulnerability exists, declining both a fix and a bounty payout while leaving the report marked high-priority.

title: Android verification is coming: Google confirms timeline and supported app stores
blurb: Google confirmed its Android developer verification system goes live September 30 in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand, requiring developers outside the Play Store to verify their identities and pay $25 or have their apps blocked from sideloading on certified devices. Verification extends to trusted third-party stores, where a developer cleared in one participating storefront is recognized on Google's side.

title: Celebrating 12 years of Project Galileo
blurb: Cloudflare marked Project Galileo's 12th anniversary by publishing its first comprehensive report on cyberattacks against civil society, drawn from telemetry across 3,400 protected domains in over 120 countries. The data shows civil society organizations faced website-exploitation attempts at more than seven times the rate of other Cloudflare customers, with DDoS the most common threat and exiled journalists hit by nearly four times the malicious traffic of journalism orgs overall.

title: GNSS Spoofing Threat for V2X communications
blurb: Researchers demonstrated undetected GNSS spoofing against V2X systems using a $300 HackRF One SDR and a coordinate-generation pipeline that forges high-fidelity GPS baseband signals at emulated speeds of 90, 145, and 200 km/h. The attack fed false positions to live Commsignia OnBoard and RoadSide Units, degrading Cooperative Awareness Message reliability without triggering detection, confirming V2X positioning has no spoofing-resistant trust anchor.

title: Malware attacks strip Roblox developers of entire games
blurb: Attackers are hijacking entire Roblox games by luring developers with fake project-manager job offers that deliver infostealer malware, including a malicious Python package called "robase." After running the file, victims lose their Roblox and Discord accounts as attackers change security settings, transfer game ownership, and republish the stolen titles for revenue.

title: Telegram admits it couldn't police exam-leak channels, India tells court
blurb: India's government told the Delhi High Court that Telegram conceded it could not proactively detect channels selling leaked NEET-UG 2026 medical-exam papers, relying instead on user reports, before the platform was blocked nationwide on June 19. The court dismissed Telegram's challenge under Section 69A of the IT Act, leaving the block in force after a BGP route leak spilled the outage as far as the UAE.

title: Appfigures: daily downloads of major VPN apps in India rose 49% on June 16 after India announced its Telegram ban; Proton and Turbo recorded largest increases (Jagmeet Singh/TechCrunch)
blurb: Daily downloads of major VPN apps in India rose 49% on June 16, the day after India announced its Telegram ban, according to Appfigures, with Proton and Turbo posting the largest gains. The surge mirrors the now-routine pattern of VPN adoption spiking after national app bans, complicating enforcement as users route around blocked services.

title: A Layered Security Framework Against Prompt Injection in RAG-Based Chatbots
blurb: This paper introduces a three-layer middleware framework that blocks both direct and indirect prompt injection in RAG chatbots without modifying the underlying LLM. Layer 1 screens input, Layer 2 enforces a provenance-based instruction hierarchy so retrieved documents cannot override operator policy, and Layer 3 audits output for policy violations and semantic drift. Across 5,080 samples on GPT-4o, Llama 3, and Mistral 7B, it cut Attack Success Rate from 71.4% to 11.3% at a 4.8% false-positive rate and 61.2ms median latency.

title: Runtime Compliance Verification for AI Agents
blurb: This paper introduces C-Trace, a framework that enforces GDPR compliance on AI agents at runtime by expressing rules like consent, purpose limitation, data minimization, and the right to erasure as formal predicates over execution traces. A runtime monitor intercepts every tool call and model output, rejecting non-compliant actions. Across four case studies, it held attack success rate at or below 12% under 10% extractor noise and 0% with perfect extraction, with false positives at or below 16%.

title: NY man charged after harassing college student with AI-generated nudes
blurb: Federal prosecutors charged 21-year-old Anthony Belford with cyberstalking after he allegedly ran a months-long harassment campaign against a former classmate, using spoofed Instagram, LinkedIn, Reddit, X, Strava, and Yahoo accounts to distribute AI-generated nude images and fabricated racist messages. Federal law bars sharing non-consensual intimate images, including synthetic ones, and the DOJ urged victims to report violations to the FBI and flag unremoved content to the FTC.

title: Your browser tab could become encrypted storage for someone else’s files
blurb: A research paper from IENYC's Gregory Magarshak describes Safecloud, a decentralized storage design where browser tabs act as encrypted storage nodes: files are chunked and encrypted on the owner's device before leaving, so storage nodes ("Drops") hold only ciphertext in IndexedDB and routing nodes ("Jets") never see keys. Every key derives from a single 32-byte root secret, and three overlapping trees handle integrity, confidentiality, and authorization over one addressing path, enabling content-addressed dedup and single-key segment streaming without exposing plaintext to any participant.

title: EU Gets a Head Start in Developing 6G Network Security
blurb: Nineteen organizations launched Shield-6G, an EU-funded project to build a shared cyber threat intelligence platform for 6G networks ahead of an expected 2030 rollout. The effort combines AI threat detection, digital twins, and honeypots to defend against a vastly larger attack surface as automation, IoT density, and embodied AI expand interconnectivity across critical infrastructure.

title: AutoTam: Specifying Secure Protocol Implementations with Tamarin Model Generation
blurb: AutoTam introduces a language-first method for formally verifying cryptographic protocol implementations, generating Tamarin prover models from a domain-specific language and proving that verified trace properties carry back to the running code. The authors pair symbolic execution for memory-safety analysis with model verification, then demonstrate the approach on signed Diffie-Hellman and a WireGuard VPN implementation that interoperates with existing clients at acceptable performance.

title: Congress tees up No FAKES Act, aiming at AI-generated deepfakes
blurb: The Senate Judiciary Committee approved the NO FAKES Act, which would grant Americans near-exclusive rights over their AI-generated likeness, enforceable for 70 years after death and carrying penalties up to $750,000 per unauthorized deepfake. The bill cleared a voice vote with backing from over 40 groups including SAG-AFTRA, though senators and free-speech advocates warn it could be weaponized to suppress legitimate speech.

title: Hostile states behind three-quarters of UK critical infrastructure attacks
blurb: NCSC CEO Richard Horne said roughly 75% of the more than 200 cyber incidents affecting UK critical national infrastructure over the past year were linked to state actors, primarily Russia, China, and Iran. Horne warned that adversaries are prepositioning footholds for disruption during conflict, citing Volt Typhoon, and said frontier AI models are accelerating attackers' ability to find vulnerabilities.

title: Asia-Pacific scam networks generate nearly $40 billion a year
blurb: INTERPOL's 2025/2026 Asia and South Pacific Cyberthreat Assessment found that industrialized scam compounds across Cambodia, Laos, Myanmar, and the Philippines generate close to $40 billion a year through investment fraud and romance scams, with some operations linked to human trafficking and forced labor. The region also logged over 135,000 ransomware attacks in 2024, including an incident that disrupted more than 280 public services tied to Indonesia's National Data Centre.

title: Low-Cost Multi-Precision Systolic Arrays for Accelerating FHE NTTs on AI ASICs
blurb: A new systolic-array design reconstructs full-precision NTT results natively, giving standard AI ASICs 1.33x faster FHE.

title: Massive breach spills credentials for thousands of sensitive networks
blurb: Researchers disclosed a breach of roughly 74,000 internet-facing Fortinet firewalls, about half of all such devices online, exposing plaintext credentials for organizations including Oracle, Chevron, Lenovo, FedEx, and a NATO defense contractor. Independent verification confirms the credentials are live, and attackers used compromised devices to reach centralized auth systems like RADIUS and Active Directory, so IT teams running FortiGate should audit for compromise immediately.

title: FortiBleed leak exposes Fortinet VPN credentials for 73,000 devices.
blurb: A data leak dubbed FortiBleed exposed Fortinet and FortiGate SSL VPN credentials, including plaintext passwords, for 73,932 firewall URLs at organizations including Chevron, Samsung, AT&T, and Mercedes-Benz. Researcher Bob Diachenko tied the cache to a Russian-speaking group that ran roughly 1.16 billion credential attempts against FortiGate targets, making credential rotation and SSL VPN review an immediate priority for affected enterprises.

title: Microsoft Confirms RoguePlanet Defender Zero-Day, Says Patch is in Development
blurb: Microsoft confirmed a privilege-escalation zero-day in the Microsoft Defender Malware Protection Engine, codenamed RoguePlanet and tracked as CVE-2026-50656 (CVSS 7.8), and said a security update is still in development. The race-condition exploit grants SYSTEM-level access regardless of whether real-time protection is enabled, and it is the fourth Defender flaw disclosed by researcher Chaotic Eclipse, leaving enterprise endpoints exposed until Microsoft ships the patch.

title: CISA Warns of Actively Exploited Joomla JCE Flaw Allowing PHP Code Execution
blurb: CISA added a maximum-severity Joomla Content Editor flaw (CVE-2026-48907, CVSS 10.0) to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after confirming active exploitation that lets unauthenticated users upload and run arbitrary PHP code. The bug affects JCE versions 1.0.0 through 2.9.99.4, was patched in 2.9.99.5 on June 3, and federal civilian agencies must apply the fix by June 19.

title: AMD silently removes memory encryption from consumer Ryzen CPUs, leaving users unaware that they may be vulnerable — security feature vanishes after newer AGESA firmware, AMD engineers go radio silent when pressed about the change
blurb: AMD quietly removed Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME) from consumer Ryzen CPUs after the AGESA 1.2.7.0 firmware update, reserving the memory-encryption feature for its Pro lineup without notifying users. The removal is undetectable on Windows and leaves non-Pro chips exposed to physical memory attacks, pushing buyers who need hardware encryption toward AMD's pricier Pro processors or commercial-tier procurement.

title: F5 Patches Two Critical NGINX Open Source Flaws Enabling Remote Code Execution
blurb: F5 patched two critical flaws in NGINX Open Source, both rated CVSS 9.2, that let remote unauthenticated attackers run code via HTTP/3 QUIC (CVE-2026-42530) and HTTP/2 proxying (CVE-2026-42055). The bugs reach across F5's NGINX portfolio, including NGINX Plus, Ingress Controller, Gateway Fabric, and App Protect, so IT teams should patch to the fixed releases or apply the HTTP/3 and header-buffer mitigations.

title: F5 issues out-of-band patches for critical NGINX vulnerabilities
blurb: F5 released out-of-band patches for two critical NGINX vulnerabilities (CVE-2026-42530 and CVE-2026-42055) that let unauthenticated remote attackers trigger denial-of-service or remote code execution on non-default configurations, affecting NGINX Plus, NGINX Open Source, Gateway Fabric, and Instance Manager. F5 flaws have a track record of active exploitation by cybercrime and nation-state groups, so admins running affected NGINX builds should patch now or apply the HTTP/3 and header-buffer mitigations.

title: Tesco moving 40,000 server workloads off VMware amid Broadcom's “abusive conduct”
blurb: Tesco is migrating 40,000 server workloads off VMware, citing "abusive conduct" by Broadcom in an ongoing UK High Court breach-of-contract suit over honoring its pre-acquisition perpetual licenses and support terms. The £73.7B retailer aims to be fully off VMware by the end of 2027 at the earliest, a forced and costly migration that signals to enterprise buyers the procurement risk of Broadcom's subscription-only licensing regime.

title: Splunk Enterprise Vulnerability Exploited in Attacks Days After Disclosure
blurb: Cisco-owned Splunk confirmed active exploitation of CVE-2026-20253, a critical unauthenticated flaw in its PostgreSQL sidecar that lets remote attackers create or truncate arbitrary files and, per WatchTowr's published PoC, achieve remote code execution. CISA added it to the KEV catalog on June 18 with a June 21 federal deadline, so IT teams running Splunk Enterprise 10.0 or 10.2 should patch to the fixed releases now.

title: Klue OAuth breach linked to 'Icarus' Salesforce data theft attacks
blurb: Market intelligence platform Klue suffered an OAuth breach that let the "Icarus" extortion group steal Salesforce CRM data from multiple organizations, with ReliaQuest and Huntress confirming attackers used Klue Battlecards integration tokens to query Salesforce's REST API for nearly 24 hours. Salesforce has disabled the Klue Battlecards connection during the investigation, leaving any enterprise relying on that integration cut off and underscoring third-party OAuth scopes as a live attack surface in CRM stacks.

title: CISA warns Fortinet users to secure devices after FortiBleed leak
blurb: CISA urged Fortinet customers to secure their devices after FortiBleed exposed plaintext VPN and firewall credentials for roughly 74,000 FortiGate appliances across 194 countries. Affected organizations including Samsung, Mercedes-Benz, Chevron, and AT&T should terminate SSL VPN and admin sessions, reset passwords, and enable phishing-resistant MFA, as threat actors are already using the leaked credentials against internet-facing devices.

title: Announcing Amazon EC2 G7 instances accelerated by NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs
blurb: AWS released its EC2 G7 instances into general availability, becoming the first major cloud provider to offer NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs paired with custom sixth-gen Intel Xeon processors. The instances deliver up to 4.6x AI inference and 2.1x graphics performance over G6, with 32GB of memory per GPU, 700 Gbps of EFA networking, and up to eight GPUs per node for inference, rendering, and VDI workloads.

title: Cybersecurity Firms Impacted by Klue Supply Chain Attack
blurb: Huntress and Recorded Future disclosed they were hit by a supply chain attack on market intelligence platform Klue, which began June 11 when attackers harvested OAuth tokens and abused the Salesforce REST API to exfiltrate CRM data, including business contacts and contract details. The incident was confined to the Klue-Salesforce integration and follows the same playbook as recent Salesloft Drift and Gainsight breaches, signaling that third-party SaaS integrations remain a soft entry point into enterprise Salesforce instances.

title: Apple fixes Beats Studio Buds flaw that let hackers spy on conversations
blurb: Apple released Beats Firmware Update 1B211 to patch a high-severity flaw (CVE-2025-20701) in Beats Studio Buds that let attackers within Bluetooth range eavesdrop through the earbud microphone without pairing or authentication. The bug sits in Airoha SoC open-source code shared across multiple vendors, so IT teams managing fleets of Bluetooth audio devices should verify the auto-delivered firmware applied across other affected Airoha-based hardware.

title: ShapedPlugin update flow hacked to infect WordPress sites
blurb: Attackers compromised WordPress plugin vendor ShapedPlugin's official update channel, pushing backdoored Pro builds of three paid plugins to paying customers between May 21 and June 16, per Wordfence. The malware installs a hidden plugin impersonating WooCommerce components that harvests admin credentials, 2FA secrets, wp-config.php database keys, and recent WooCommerce payment data, giving operators remote file-writing access on infected sites.

title: Police cleans nearly 15,000 SocGholish-infected sites tied to Evil Corp
blurb: Law enforcement agencies from the Netherlands, Canada, the US, and Germany cleaned SocGholish malware from 14,971 compromised WordPress sites and took 106 servers and domains offline, in an Operation Endgame action targeting the infection chain tied to Russian cybercrime group Evil Corp. The cleanup applies to the JavaScript downloader, active since 2017, that hijacks legitimate sites to push fake browser updates and has deployed Dridex, DoppelPaymer, and Evil Corp ransomware.

title: Salesforce Disables Klue App Integration After OAuth Token Abuse Exposes Customer Data
blurb: Salesforce disabled the Klue Battlecards integration on June 11 after attackers stole OAuth tokens from Klue's compromised integration infrastructure and used them to reach customer Salesforce data, including records belonging to Huntress. The breach, attributed to the Icarus extortion group, hit a third-party app connection rather than the Salesforce platform itself, putting the spotlight on OAuth token governance for connected enterprise apps.

title: FortiBleed campaign exposes 75,000 Fortinet firewalls worldwide
blurb: Researchers disclosed FortiBleed, a credential-harvesting campaign that exposed roughly 75,000 internet-facing Fortinet FortiGate firewalls, about half of those visible on Shodan, across 194 countries and more than 21,000 domains. Attackers collected configuration files to recover working administrator and SSL VPN credentials, giving them persistent access that survives patching and forcing affected enterprises into full credential rotation rather than a simple update.

title: DragonForce Hackers Abuse Microsoft Teams Relays to Hide Backdoor.Turn C2 Traffic
blurb: DragonForce ransomware affiliates deployed a custom Go-based RAT, Backdoor.Turn, that tunnels command-and-control traffic through Microsoft's legitimate Teams TURN relay infrastructure, leaving defenders to see only outbound connections to trusted Microsoft servers. The technique, documented by Symantec and Carbon Black after a one-to-two-month intrusion at a major US services firm, defeats network monitoring that allowlists Microsoft domains and pressures IT teams to inspect Teams relay traffic they previously trusted.

title: 15,000 WordPress Websites Cleaned Up in SocGholish Botnet Takedown
blurb: A law-enforcement operation across the Netherlands, Canada, the US, and Germany, backed by Europol, took down 106 command-and-control servers and cleaned malware from 14,971 infected WordPress sites running the SocGholish loader. Active since 2017 and tied to the Evil Corp-linked broker TA569, SocGholish exposed roughly 55% of cloud customers this year, so IT teams should force credential resets and MFA on any compromised CMS accounts.

title: Gentlemen ransomware uses multiple EDR killers to disable defenses
blurb: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service operation is actively maintaining a suite of EDR killers, led by a custom tool ESET dubbed GentleKiller that has at least eight variants impersonating products like Kaspersky and CrowdStrike. The tool uses the bring-your-own-vulnerable-driver technique to gain kernel privileges and disable more than 400 processes across roughly 48 security vendors, including Microsoft, SentinelOne, Palo Alto, and Sophos.

title: Sweeping Credential-Harvesting Heist Compromises +30K Fortinet Devices
blurb: A Russian-speaking threat group has compromised more than 30,791 internet-facing Fortinet firewalls and VPN gateways across 194 countries, harvesting verified working credentials rather than exploiting any Fortinet flaw, according to SOCRadar. Telecommunications, government, and critical-infrastructure organizations dominate the victim list, and over 20% of affected devices belong to enterprises with more than $1B in annual revenue, putting weak default and long-lived admin accounts squarely in scope for credential audits.

title: Massive password-stealing attack hits 75k Fortinet firewalls
blurb: Attackers compromised roughly 75,000 internet-facing Fortinet firewalls in a coordinated campaign to harvest administrator credentials, exposing the network perimeter that enterprises rely on FortiGate appliances to defend. IT teams running exposed FortiOS management interfaces face urgent credential rotation and patch validation, and the scale puts renewed scrutiny on edge-device hardening across any stack standardized on Fortinet.

title: Top announcements of the AWS Summit in New York, 2026
blurb: AWS used its New York Summit keynote to expand Amazon Bedrock AgentCore, adding a managed Knowledge Base for enterprise RAG, a fully managed web search tool, and the now generally available AgentCore harness for config-driven agent deployment. AWS also added a WAF capability that lets publishers meter and charge AI bots for content access, and previewed AWS Context, a service that maps enterprise data into a knowledge graph.

title: AWS Security Agent adds threat modeling, Kiro power and Claude Code plugin, and more
blurb: AWS expanded its Security Agent, now part of AWS Continuum, with preview threat modeling that applies the STRIDE framework to design docs and source code, plus pull request scanning, remediation, and new GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and Confluence integrations. A Claude Code plugin, Kiro support, and an open MCP integration let teams run code reviews and generate threat models from any AI-powered IDE or CLI without leaving their workflow.

title: Authorities disrupt Evil Corp’s SocGholish botnet
blurb: A multinational law enforcement effort disrupted SocGholish, the "FakeUpdates" malware framework linked to Russian group Evil Corp, seizing 106 servers and remediating nearly 15,000 infected sites across the US, Canada, Germany, and the Netherlands. SocGholish served as an initial-access broker for ransomware including LockBit, RansomHub, and DoppelPaymer since 2017, so the takedown cuts off a supply line that fed multiple downstream extortion campaigns.

title: Tesco is sprinting to quit VMware and Broadcom despite rapid migration risks
blurb: Tesco is accelerating its exit from VMware and Broadcom, pushing an aggressive migration timeline despite the operational risk of moving production workloads quickly off the virtualization stack.
